Auto Stream
excerpt: What Auto Stream Does Auto Stream is a GCXONE feature that automatically opens live camera feeds when an intrusion alarm is triggered at a site. It is built for environments where alarm systems (such as motion detectors, door contacts, or other senso
content:
What Auto Stream Does Auto Stream is a GCXONE feature that automatically opens live camera feeds when an intrusion alarm is triggered at a site. It is built for environments where alarm systems (such as motion detectors, door contacts, or other sensors) are connected to Talos but don’t provide video on their own. When an alarm is triggered, Auto Stream instantly starts streaming the cameras linked to the affected zone. This gives operators immediate visual context without needing to search for cameras or take any manual action. The correct cameras appear automatically in the Video Viewer.
Why It Matters In many real-world setups, alarms and video operate separately. An operator gets an alert—but still has to find the right cameras manually. That takes time, especially during high-pressure situations. Auto Stream removes that friction. By automatically linking alarms to video feeds, operators can immediately see what’s happening on-site. This improves response speed, reduces manual effort, and helps teams make faster, more confident decisions.
How It Works When an alarm triggers, Talos sends a message through the Genesis-Talos Bridge to GCXONE. GCXONE identifies the cameras mapped to that zone and streams them immediately. Depending on the scenario:
Matching zone found → Only cameras mapped to that zone are streamed
No matching zone or no zone in alarm → The entire site is streamed
Multiple alarms → Cameras from all relevant zones are combined (no duplicates)
Motion alarm present → Motion alarms take priority and switch to quad view
New alarms during workflow → The stream updates automatically in real time Prerequisites: Before enabling Auto Stream, confirm the following are in place:
Genesis-Talos Bridge extension is installed and active
NXGEN Genesis integration is enabled in Talos
Auto Stream alarm codes are configured in Talos (typically BA or BA 130) Configuration Steps To configure Auto Stream, follow these steps: Step 1 — Enable the NXGEN Genesis Integration In Talos, go to Company → Integrations and confirm that the NXGEN Genesis integration is enabled. Talos Company Integrations tab showing available integrations _Figure 1: Talos Integrations Tab - Confirm the NXGEN Genesis integration is enabled before configuring Auto Stream._
NXGEN Genesis integration shown as Enabled in Talos _Figure 2: NXGEN Genesis Integration - The integration must show as Enabled for Auto Stream triggers to reach GCXONE._ Step 2 — Configure Alarm Codes Within the NXGEN Genesis integration settings, define which alarm codes should trigger Auto Stream. Use BA or BA 130, which are the standard codes for intrusion panel alarms. Step 3 — Create Zones in Talos For each site:
Navigate to the site in Talos
Open the Zones and Users tab
Add zones with a Zone ID and Zone Name Always create zones in Talos before configuring them in GCXONE. _Figure 3: Talos Site Overview - The Zones and Users tab is accessible from the top navigation within the site._ _Figure 4: Add a new zone in the Zones and Users tab._
Zones and Users configuration table in Talos showing Zone Type, Zone ID, and Zone Name columns _Figure 5: Zones and Users Configuration - Zone IDs must be created here before assigning sensors in GCXONE._ Step 4 — Assign Zones to Cameras in GCXONE In GCXONE:
Open the Edit Sensor dialog for each camera
Go to the Properties tab
Enter the corresponding Zone ID in the Zone field Multiple cameras can share the same Zone ID if they cover the same area. Edit Sensor dialog in GCXONE showing the Properties tab with the Zone field _Figure 6: Edit Sensor Dialog - Enter the Zone ID in the Properties tab to link the camera to a Talos zone._ Cameras on different devices within the same site can share the same zone ID. All matching cameras stream together when the alarm arrives.
Key Capabilities
Automatically starts camera streams when alarms are triggered
Uses zone mapping to show only relevant cameras
Combines multiple alarm zones into a single stream
Handles motion alarm priority intelligently
Updates streams dynamically as new alarms arrive
Real-World Use Cases
A burglary alarm triggers at Zone 3 of a warehouse. Auto Stream opens only the cameras in that zone for an immediate view.
An alarm arrives with no zone data. Auto Stream streams all cameras at the site so no area goes unchecked.
Two alarms trigger at different zones. Auto Stream combines both zone camera lists with no duplicates.
A motion alarm and a burglary alarm arrive together. Quad view takes priority; Auto Stream continues on a secondary screen.
Best Practices
Always create zones in Talos before configuring them in GCXONE
Make sure all cameras are correctly mapped to their respective zones
Confirm that the correct alarm codes are configured to trigger Auto Stream
Periodically review zone mappings to avoid outdated or incorrect setups
Additional Details Streaming Behavior: The table below shows how Auto Stream responds to each alarm scenario: Troubleshooting:
Entire site streams even

title: HealthCheck
excerpt:
What HealthCheck Does HealthCheck automatically monitors every connected camera in your fleet. It verifies each camera is reachable, streaming, and delivering a valid image. The feature is built around three goals:
Validate that all cameras are onlin
content:
What HealthCheck Does HealthCheck automatically monitors every connected camera in your fleet. It verifies each camera is reachable, streaming, and delivering a valid image. The feature is built around three goals:
Validate that all cameras are online and functioning correctly.
Deliver proactive alerts for failed or degraded feeds.
Give operators flexible control via scheduling, manual execution, and hierarchy-based configuration.
Why It Matters Large camera networks fail silently. Cameras go offline, get obstructed, or get tampered with. No one notices until an incident exposes the gap. HealthCheck eliminates that risk.
Camera connectivity loss or total offline status.
Blocked, obstructed, or vandalized lenses.
Low-light or night conditions making video unusable.
Overexposed or washed-out images from glare or direct light.
Camera movement or deliberate tampering.
Software-level failures - no license, no signal. Without automated monitoring, these issues go undetected. Coverage gaps grow until an incident exposes them. _Figure 1: HealthCheck -what it does, productivity impact, vulnerabilities closed and growth impact_
How It Works When HealthCheck runs, the platform executes the following steps:
Connects to the camera live stream
Captures a snapshot from the feed
Analyzes the image against configured checks
Generates a health verdict, healthy, low light, obstructed, and more
Records and displays results in the platform interface HealthChecks run automatically on a schedule or on demand. Operators can trigger manual checks from the interface at any time. HealthCheck Modes Three monitoring modes are available, each designed for a different level of operational need: Key Capabilities
Continuous Camera Monitoring: Periodically verifies each camera is reachable and delivering a valid stream
Automatic Failure Detection: Identifies cameras that are offline, unreachable, or delivering degraded feeds
Image Condition Analysis: Detects low light, glare, obstructions, and conditions affecting video usability
AI-Based Anomaly Detection: In Advanced mode, AI models analyze snapshots to detect tampering, tilt, and obstruction
Flexible Scheduling: Run checks automatically at configurable intervals or trigger them on demand
Hierarchical Configuration: Apply settings at any level of the platform hierarchy, from Service Provider down to individual sensors
Centralized Dashboard: Color-coded indicators give instant visibility into healthy and failed cameras across all sites Visual Indicators and Status Display The HealthCheck dashboard is in the left sidebar (eye icon). Summary cards show checked cameras, healthy devices, connection failures, and detected issues. Cameras display in grid or list view. Each shows its health status and last check timestamp. Use filters to narrow results quickly:
Status filter: Healthy, Failed, or No Reference Image
Camera Type filter: Narrow results to a specific device or sensor type
Include Inactive Sensors: Show cameras that are not currently active
Include Excluded Sensors: Show cameras manually excluded from HealthCheck
Generate Report: Create a downloadable HealthCheck report for the selected customer Camera Status States _Figure 2: HealthCheck Dashboard - summary cards._ _Figure 3: HealthCheck list view._ _Figure 4: Camera detail view ._
Real-World Use Cases
Fleet Health at Scale: A monitoring center manages 500+ cameras across 50 sites. HealthCheck runs hourly checks and flags offline cameras before the morning shift. Technicians are dispatched before any operator notices a gap.
Tamper Detection: A perimeter camera is physically repositioned overnight. Plus tier detects the angle deviation and triggers an alarm. The issue is resolved before the next incident.
Maintenance Windows: A site undergoes scheduled network maintenance. The operator pauses HealthCheck for affected devices. False alarms are suppressed and the alarm queue stays clean.
Compliance Reporting: A customer requires monthly proof that all cameras were operational. HealthCheck generates the report automatically and delivers it on schedule.
Best Practices
Use Advanced mode for high-security or high-value deployments where predictive monitoring matters
Configure HealthChecks at the Customer or Site level to ensure full coverage without configuring each camera individually
Update Tampering and Tilt reference images after any scene change, repositioning, or infrastructure work
Disable on-screen overlays on cameras enrolled in Advanced mode to improve AI accuracy
Use on-demand checks after any camera maintenance, repositioning, or infrastructure change
Monitor the HealthCheck dashboard regularly and set up alert notifications for Camera.health failures to catch issues early.
Additional Details Advanced Analysis Pipeline In Advanced mode, each snapshot runs through an ordered sequence of checks. The pipeline stops at the first detected issue. :::note Important Only the highest-priority issue detected is reported per camera per chec

title: Live View Advanced Troubleshooting
excerpt:
Live View Advanced Troubleshooting This guide helps you resolve advanced Live View issues related to devices, network configuration, and platform behavior in GCXONE.
General Checks If a video stream is not loading, start with basic validation:
Verify
content:
Live View Advanced Troubleshooting This guide helps you resolve advanced Live View issues related to devices, network configuration, and platform behavior in GCXONE.
General Checks If a video stream is not loading, start with basic validation:
Verify the device is online in the Device Explorer
Check local network connectivity and internet access
Restart the camera or device
Confirm device credentials and network configuration
Ensure the device is reachable on the network
Device-Specific Issues Some devices require additional configuration to enable stable live streaming.
Avigilon
Open UDP ports 51000-55000 between viewing stations and servers
Set "Connection Type" to WAN (Secured) in Site Networking settings
Hikvision
Enable UPnP on both the router and the device, or configure port forwarding
Enable ISAPI protocol for remote access
Ensure the user account has Operator permissions or higher
Dahua
Ensure sufficient bandwidth is available (minimum 100 Mbps recommended for multiple streams)
Verify the viewing workstation meets performance requirements (multi-core CPU, sufficient RAM)
Uniview
Reduce camera resolution for sub-streams (for example, D1)
Configure the NVR to prioritize sub-streams for live viewing
Axis
Verify the camera is enabled in system settings
Confirm compatibility with the operating system and browser environment
Camect
Use the secondary RTSP profile (sub-stream) to reduce bandwidth usage and improve stability
Network and Port Requirements Live View depends on proper network configuration and open communication ports. Standard required ports:
RTSP (554)
HTTP (80)
HTTPS (443) Additional ports may be required depending on the device:
Dahua control port (37777)
Senstar RTSP port (8488)
Heitel HTConnect port (3333) Ensure all required ports are open and not blocked by the firewall. Whitelist GCXONE service endpoints on the customer network if required.
Software and Interface Issues Some issues are caused by user interface or configuration settings.
Display Issues - Avoid browser-based translation tools. Use built-in language settings instead
PTZ Not Working - Verify user permissions and confirm the device is configured as a PTZ camera
Auto Streaming Not Triggered - Ensure required integrations and settings are enabled
Proactive Monitoring Use built-in tools to detect issues before they impact operations.
Health Checks - Configure periodic checks to verify camera availability and stream accessibility
Advanced Monitoring - Use AI-based detection to identify blur, obstruction, or degraded video quality
Diagnostic Tools Use external tools to isolate the source of the issue.
Test streams using manufacturer-specific clients
If the stream works in the native client but not in GCXONE, the issue may be platform-related
If the stream fails in both, the issue is likely device or network related

title: Maps
excerpt: What Maps Does Maps gives you a unified geospatial view of your entire security estate. Operators can visualize customers, sites, devices, cameras, and sensors on a live satellite or road map and interact with them directly without leaving the map sc
content:
What Maps Does Maps gives you a unified geospatial view of your entire security estate. Operators can visualize customers, sites, devices, cameras, and sensors on a live satellite or road map and interact with them directly without leaving the map screen. Maps integrates with Alarm Handling through the Multi-Monitor feature, giving operators instant location context the moment an alarm is assigned.
Why It Matters Managing multiple sites across different locations creates orientation delays during incident response. Switching between screens to find a site slows everything down. Maps solves this by keeping all location context visible and live. When paired with Alarm Handling, the map follows the alarm automatically, no manual searching, no screen switching.
Key Capabilities How It Works Before a site appears on the map, it must be created and configured with its geographic coordinates. This section walks through the complete setup workflow. Site Overview Page Navigate to Configuration→ Sites→ Overview to access a site’s overview page. The Overview tab is the landing page for any site and gives you a summary of its status. _Figure 1- Site Overview Page_ The Overview page displays:
Basic Info: Contact number for the site
Location Info: Country, city, postal code, and street address
Site Statistics: Active devices, non-active devices, total devices, and total sensors
:::tip A site must have valid latitude and longitude values set before it will appear as a pin on the global Map view. ::: Editing Site Details Click the Edit button in the top-right of the Overview page to open the Edit Site dialog. The dialog has two tabs: Edit and Properties. Contact Information The Contact Information section within the Edit tab lets you capture the site's email address and phone number.
3624fdd5bf7676a18af9d0c263f6c5b9d09f33bd.png _Figure 2 - Edit Site dialog showing the Contact Information and Location Details sections._ Location Details The Location Details section is where you set the site's physical coordinates. Fill in the following fields: bc1241ed133d8ad9be54beac60c18c3ff6a96941.png _Figure 3 - Location Details filled in with latitude 31.9489113 and longitude 35.8928442._
:::note Important Coordinates must be entered in decimal degree format. Do not use degrees/minutes/seconds notation. Once saved, the site pin will appear on the Map view at the specified location. ::: Accessing the Map View The global Map view is accessible from the left-hand navigation bar. It displays all customers and their sites as pins on a world map.
028cbebfba7fed1ceb1ac720033bad180160d190.png _Figure 4 - Map view with the search bar open. Sites without coordinates set will not appear in search results._ Use the search bar at the top-left of the map to locate a specific site by name. As you type, matching site names appear in a dropdown. Selecting a result pans the map to that site.
:::tip If a site does not appear in search results, verify that its latitude and longitude have been saved correctly in the Edit Site dialog. ::: 8c10416524f2b76463202cd95ba80c27352269aa.png _Figure 5 - Map view zoomed to Amman, Jordan, with "Map Demo" appearing as a search suggestion after typing "ma"._ The map supports two view modes:
Map: Standard road map with street names and labels
Satellite: High-resolution aerial imagery for precise site placement Map Edit Mode Edit Mode lets operators and administrators enrich the map with site-specific overlays including cameras, IO sensors, towers, zone boundaries, and text labels. These overlays persist on the map and give control room staff at-a-glance situational awareness. Entering Edit Mode Navigate to Map view, locate your site, and click the Edit Mode button in the top-right corner of the map canvas.
3e717e1793b4bbe53653a2e0310fdfdfd204b082.png _Figure 6 - The Edit Mode toggle button._ Once activated, the toolbar at the bottom of the screen expands to show all available placement tools, and a zone-drawing panel appears on the left.
be4a5e3e45d43c3c6d2f6ad43919489068c5883e.png _Figure 7 - Map in Edit Mode._ Drawing Zones Use the zone drawing panel on the left to mark defined areas on the map. Three shapes are available:
Square: Click and drag to draw a rectangular zone
Circle: Click and drag to draw a circular zone
Polygon: Click multiple points to define a custom boundary, then close the shape to complete it
:::tip Zones are ideal for highlighting restricted areas, patrol routes, or logical site sections such as "Zone A" or "Perimeter". ::: Adding a Camera

title: NOVA99x
excerpt: What NOVA99x Does NOVA99x is GCXONE's intelligent false-alarm filtering module designed for modern surveillance operations. It analyzes every motion-triggered event in real time using computer vision, determining whether the event represents a real t
content: What NOVA99x Does NOVA99x is GCXONE's intelligent false-alarm filtering module designed for modern surveillance operations. It analyzes every motion-triggered event in real time using computer vision, determining whether the event represents a real threat or environmental noise before it ever reaches an operator. By filtering out up to 99% of false alarms, NOVA99x allows security teams to scale operations without increasing headcount. Unlike traditional systems that pass all alarms downstream, NOVA99x operates inline within the alarm pipeline, ensuring that only validated and actionable events are delivered to operators. Why It Matters Every operator knows this problem. Most motion alarms are not real threats. They come from swaying trees, passing headlights, animals, or changing weather conditions. Over time, this constant noise leads to alert fatigue, making it harder to stay focused and increasing the risk of missing real incidents. NOVA99x removes that noise before it reaches humans. Instead of reviewing hundreds of low-value alarms, operators only see events that have already been validated by AI, improving response quality, reducing workload, and protecting attention during critical moments. How It Works NOVA99x processes every motion-triggered event the moment it is generated, running it through a real-time computer vision pipeline. Each event is classified as a true alarm or a false alarm before it reaches an operator. True alarms are passed through. False alarms are suppressed automatically. This happens instantly and adds no latency to the alarm workflow. Open NOVA99x from the dashboard: Adjust the time range, compare real vs. false alarms, and filter events by camera type (thermal or optical) to quickly understand what’s happening across your system. NOVA99x runs three core algorithms to achieve this: Advanced Object Detector achieves state-of-the-art performance in real-world surveillance conditions, identifying relevant objects with high accuracy. Dynamic Motion Detection pinpoints meaningful movement within an event and filters out environmental noise like shadows, reflections, and weather artifacts. Tracking and Re-Identification (ReID) adds temporal context to each event, filtering out stationary objects like parked vehicles that would otherwise trigger repeated false alarms. Key Capabilities NOVA99x operates at every level of the GCXONE entity hierarchy. Lower-level entities automatically inherit configuration from higher levels unless explicitly overridden. A single service-provider-level policy can cover entire operations with zero additional configuration. Real-World Use Cases Service Provider-Wide Noise Control A service provider deploys NOVA99x at the top level, enforcing a baseline alarm quality standard across all customers. No single noisy deployment can overwhelm the shared operations team. Customer-Level Alarm Policies Filtering is tailored per customer based on risk tolerance or contractual requirements, without affecting any other customer. Site-Level Environmental Filtering A site with complex lighting or high foot traffic gets its own configuration to handle unique environmental conditions without touching the rest of the system. Device and Sensor Precision Control A problematic camera is fine-tuned individually. No impact on the rest of the site. Large-Scale Expansion During rapid onboarding, NOVA99x stabilizes alarm volume from day one, preventing early overload and maintaining quality as the operation grows. Best Practices To achieve optimal performance, consider the following best practices: Optical (RGB) cameras: Verify all cameras meet the minimum technical requirements before activating. Detection accuracy depends directly on image quality at the sensor level. Thermal cameras: Configure the sensor as Thermal in GCXONE before activating NOVA99x. This activates the correct detection model. Keep tall heat-emitting objects like chimneys and vents out of the field of view as they can resemble a human profile. If excessive contrast enhancement introduces false detections, enable useContrastEnhancement for the sensor. Note that cats and small animals share similar heat profiles to humans and may occasionally be misclassified. Night detection: Cameras without IR are best suited for indoor use. Outdoor deployment may require additional lighting. Cameras with IR night vision have a reduced effective field of view in low-light mode, requiring a minimum bounding box of 600 px² for person detection. High-speed vehicle scenarios: For cameras monitoring fast-moving or turning vehicles, set carBypassDisplacementCheck = true. This bypasses ReID and uses motion-box intersection instead. Do not apply this setting to parking lot cameras as it reduces jitter filtering. Additional Details Activat

title: Audio Routing & Conference Mode
excerpt: What Audio Routing and Conference Mode Does Audio Routing and Conference Mode allow operators to communicate with field devices and other operators in real time. The system supports flexible audio routing - from one-to-one communication to full multi
content:
What Audio Routing and Conference Mode Does Audio Routing and Conference Mode allow operators to communicate with field devices and other operators in real time. The system supports flexible audio routing - from one-to-one communication to full multi-device collaboration - depending on how devices are configured.
Why It Matters During live incidents, the wrong person hearing the wrong thing - or nothing at all - can make a bad situation worse. Operators need to issue instructions, verify situations, and coordinate responses without delay. Proper audio routing ensures communication reaches the right devices and people at the right time.
How It Works Audio routing is controlled by the Active status of configured audio devices at each site. GCXONE uses a Twilio Conference Bridge to handle multi-party sessions, ensuring high-quality, low-latency duplex communication. Based on device configuration, the platform automatically determines how audio is routed between operators and devices.
Key Capabilities
Real-Time Communication - Enable live audio between operators and field devices.
Multi-Device Routing - Broadcast audio to multiple devices simultaneously.
Direct Communication - Connect to a single device for focused interaction.
Conference Mode - Allow multiple operators and devices to participate in a shared audio session.
Cloud-Based Audio - Handle audio routing through a centralized cloud service. No Local Mode required.
Audio Routing Modes Audio behavior depends on how devices are configured at the site.
Conference Mode - All active devices and operators are connected via a central conference bridge. Best for command centers or guard booths with multiple intercoms and speakers that need to hear the same broadcast simultaneously.
Direct Mode - The operator connects to a single active device. All other hardware is ignored. Best for single-point intercoms or dedicated phone-line integrations.
Device Audio Mode - The system uses the camera's built-in microphone and speaker via G.711 or AAC over RTSP when no external devices are active. Best for basic cameras with built-in audio but no external speaker system.
Real-World Use Cases
An operator broadcasts a warning message across multiple speakers during an active incident.
A guard communicates directly with a single intercom at a site entrance.
A control room coordinates with multiple devices simultaneously during a live security event.
A site falls back to built-in camera audio when no external audio devices are available.
Best Practices
Activate only the devices needed for the current scenario - unnecessary active devices create noise and confusion.
Use Conference Mode for coordinated responses across multiple locations.
Use Direct Mode when focused one-to-one communication matters more than broadcast reach.
Ensure users have the REMOTE_TWO_WAY_AUDIO permission enabled before initiating audio - discovering a permissions gap during an incident costs time.
Monitor bandwidth when running multi-device audio sessions. High-fidelity audio adds approximately 64 kbps per participant - ensure your network has sufficient overhead.
Configuration Overview Audio configuration involves three steps:
Register a Genesis Audio device in GCXONE. Navigate to Configuration, select your site, go to the Devices tab, click Add, and set the type to Genesis Audio. Enter a name and click Discover. The system generates unique SIP credentials - use these to configure your IP speaker or SIP horn.
Activate devices based on the desired routing mode. In the site tree, select the Genesis Audio device and toggle the Active switch to ON. Repeat for all devices you want to include in the session.
Initiate an audio session from the Video Viewer. Drag a sensor or camera into a tile, locate the Mic icon - default state is red - and click it to start the call. The icon turns blue once the session is active.
Additional Details
Permissions - Audio features require the REMOTE_TWO_WAY_AUDIO permission enabled on the user account.
Network Impact - High-fidelity audio adds approximately 64 kbps per participant. Plan for sufficient bandwidth overhead in multi-party sessions.
Cloud Handling - Audio is managed through the cloud via a SIP gateway and does not require Local Mode to be active.
Device Support - External audio devices and camera audio capabilities may vary by manufacturer.

content:
Local Mode Configuration To leverage P2P streaming and local SDK features, the Local Mode service must be installed on every operator workstation.
Installation Steps
Download the Installer: Obtain the latest Genesis Installer (v.2.8.2 or later)
Run as Administrator: Right-click Genesis-Installer.exe and select Run as Administrator
Follow On-Screen Instructions: Complete the wizard to install the Local Mode service components
Verify Service: Ensure the Genesis Local Mode service is running in the Windows Services manager (Services.msc)
Enabling Local Mode in GCXONE Once the service is installed locally, you must ensure it is enabled in your platform settings:
Navigate to the Configuration App
Go to Sites and select your target site
Under the Settings or General tab, ensure the Enable Local Mode toggle is active
Troubleshooting Connectivity If Local Mode features such as P2P streaming are not working:
Ping Test: Verify the workstation can reach the NVR or Device IP
Port Check: Ensure local firewalls allow traffic on the device's SDK ports such as 80, 8000, and 37777
Service Refresh: Restart the Genesis Local Mode service on the workstation Important: Local Mode settings are NOT required for Genesis Audio (SIP) calls, as they are initiated directly via the cloud.

title: Local Mode Overview
excerpt:
Local Mode Overview Local Mode allows the operator workstation to establish a direct, peer-to-peer (P2P) connection to a Network Video Recorder (NVR) or device, typically bypassing the central cloud streaming gateway for high-bandwidth functions.
Key
content:
Local Mode Overview Local Mode allows the operator workstation to establish a direct, peer-to-peer (P2P) connection to a Network Video Recorder (NVR) or device, typically bypassing the central cloud streaming gateway for high-bandwidth functions.
Key Functionalities When fully supported by a device type, Local Mode enables the following features directly on the operator's workstation:
P2P Streaming — Fast, direct video streaming with minimal latency
Live Video — Real-time video monitoring bypassing cloud proxies
Playback — Review of archived footage directly from local device storage
Local SDK Audio — High-performance two-way audio and announcements via device SDK
Encrypted Streams — Handling of encrypted communication streams from the device
Local PTZ & I/O — Direct control of pan-tilt-zoom and relay operations
Applicability Local Mode is highly recommended for sites with multiple operator workstations and high camera counts to maintain local bandwidth efficiency.
Not Supported Device Types Local Mode is not supported or required for:
Hanwha-Techwin NVRs: All features managed via cloud integration
Genesis Audio (SIP/Twilio): Cloud-native by design
InnoVi AI Cloud: Purely cloud-based SaaS solution
Senstar / Viasys: Integration is exclusively cloud-based
Prerequisites
Admin Privileges: Required for installation of the Local Mode service
Network Routing: Workstations must have network access to the device IPs
Genesis Installer: The Local Mode service must be installed on every operator workstation

content:
Platform Feature List GCXONE centralizes video monitoring, alarm processing, and system management into a single platform. This page covers every capability, organized by function.
Video Management Operators spend most of their time in video. These features make sure that time is as efficient as possible.
Live View - Monitor all connected cameras in real time using customizable multi-camera layouts.
P2P Streaming - Direct peer-to-peer video delivery with ultra-low latency. No cloud relay required. Supported by Dahua and Hikvision.
GreenStream - Automatically adjusts stream resolution based on viewport size. Reduces CPU, GPU, and bandwidth usage. Supported by all manufacturers.
GPU Dewarping - Correct fisheye distortion on the client side without server processing overhead.
Archived Playback - Access and review recorded footage for investigations and audits.
Timeline View - Navigate historical footage using a chronological visual timeline.
Time-Lapse - Convert hours of footage into a fast, reviewable summary clip. Supported by all cloud sites.
PTZ Controls - Control camera pan, tilt, zoom, and point-to-center remotely. Supported by Milestone, Dahua, and Hikvision.
AI and Alarm Processing Alarm noise is the biggest drain on operator performance. These features exist to cut it.
Human Detection - Trigger alarms only when a person is detected. Reduces false alarms by up to 80%.
NOVA99x - Eliminates false alarms at the source using advanced detection logic.
ZenMode - Groups multiple alarm events into a single review video. Reduces handling time to 2-3 minutes and filters up to 60% of low-priority alarms.
SLA Enforcement - Enforces a 60-90 second response window for all priority alerts, keeping operations compliant.
Event Metadata - Adds bounding boxes and labels for people and vehicles to speed up alarm review.
Redundancy Timer - Suppresses duplicate events from the same source within a defined time window. Default is 30 seconds.
Event Overflow Protection - Automatically suspends noisy sensors for 5 minutes to protect operators and system stability.
Device Management Managing hundreds of devices manually does not scale. These features handle it for you.
Automatic Discovery - Detect and register devices and sensors automatically after initial setup.
Hierarchical Organization - Structure assets as Tenant - Customer - Site - Device - Sensor for scalable management.
BulkImport - Deploy multiple sites and devices from a spreadsheet. Eliminates manual configuration at scale.
TowerGuard - Monitor power, connectivity, storage, and device health across all tower deployments from one interface.
Device Move - Transfer devices between sites within the same tenant without reconfiguration.
TimeSync - Align device timestamps automatically for accurate event correlation across all sites.
Health Monitoring Problems caught early cost less than problems discovered by customers. These features keep you ahead of failures.
HealthCheck Basic - Run automated camera checks twice daily with live feed verification and basic status reporting.
HealthCheck Advanced - Configure heartbeat checks between 15 minutes and 8 hours. Detect network drops proactively.
HealthCheck Plus - Perform advanced diagnostics with AI-powered obstruction detection and fleet analytics.
Tag-Based Exclusion - Exclude cameras from checks using custom tags such as "Under Maintenance". Automatically resume after a defined period.
Communication and I/O Some situations require more than watching. These features let operators act directly from the platform.
Genesis Audio (SIP) - Enable two-way audio using IP speakers with SIP and Twilio integration.
Custom SIP URLs - Connect legacy SIP hardware using dedicated audio endpoints.
Digital I/O - Control gates, barriers, and sirens in real time from the interface.
ARM/DISARM Control - Schedule site arming and disarming without on-site access.
IoT and Energy Telemetry Remote and off-grid deployments need more than camera monitoring. These features cover the full picture.
Victron Integration - Track solar yield, battery voltage, and charger status in real time.
Teltonika Fleet - Monitor GPS location and network health for mobile security towers.
Reconeyez Synergy - Monitor battery level and LTE signal strength for remote intrusion panels.
Platform Administration Scale brings complexity. These features keep it under control.
Multi-Tenant Architecture - Isolate data per client using subdomains with full logical separation between tenants.
Role-Based Access Control - Assign granular permissions to Installers, Administrators, and Operators.
Map Interface - Visualize camera locations with zone definition and geo-fencing support.
Catalog Repository - Maintain a centralized registry of all supported hardware gateways, sensors, and intelligent services.
Audit Trails - Track every user action and configuration change with immutable logs, supporting compliance and forensic review.
Reporting and Analytics Data without context is noise. These

content:
What PulseView Does PulseView takes your live camera feeds and turns them into professional timelapse videos — automatically. You pick the camera and the timeframe, and PulseView handles everything else. No editing software, no manual scrubbing, no extra steps. Just clean, ready-to-share output. It's built for long-term projects and site monitoring, giving operators, admins, and clients immediate visual context without sitting through hours of footage.
Why It Matters Admins deal with footage requests constantly — and every request means manual review. That time adds up faster than most teams realize. PulseView eliminates that entirely. Instead of spending hours pulling and reviewing footage, you get compliance-ready visuals automatically, with professional output that holds up in client meetings and audits. For organizations running regular reporting cycles, that kind of efficiency translates to real savings — €10k+ annually for many teams.
How It Works Select the camera you want, define the timeframe, and let PulseView do the rest. It processes the footage and generates the timelapse automatically — no editing required, no back-and-forth, no waiting. The result is professional-grade video that's immediately ready to share or review.
Key Capabilities

Zero Editing Required: The timelapse is created instantly — what used to take hours now takes seconds
Professional-Grade Output: High-quality video ready for client reporting, compliance documentation, and stakeholder presentations
Built for Long-Term Projects: Ideal for construction sites, infrastructure projects, and any scenario that requires ongoing site monitoring
Visual Reporting: Delivers the visual context your team needs to support compliance requirements and keep clients informed
Real-World Use Cases
A project manager needs to show a client three months of construction progress. Instead of compiling footage manually, PulseView puts together a clean timelapse in seconds — ready to present on the spot.
A security provider faces a compliance audit. Rather than reviewing hours of recordings, they generate timelapse evidence through PulseView that meets documentation requirements immediately.
An operations team monitors infrastructure across multiple sites over an extended period. PulseView produces visual summaries that make it easy to spot trends and report progress to leadership — without anyone having to sit through raw footage.
An admin team that handles frequent footage requests from stakeholders cuts the time spent per request from hours to seconds, freeing the team up for work that actually needs their attention.

Operators see real-time status across the entire fleet, get alerted the moment something goes wrong, and respond before issues escalate.
How It Works TowerGuard collects real-time data from connected devices at each tower site, tracking power, connectivity, storage, and device health. title: TimeSync
excerpt: What TimeSync Does TimeSync automatically synchronizes the time and timezone of every connected device across all your sites. It pushes the correct settings continuously keeping every camera, alarm, and log locked to one verified timeline, with no ma
content:
What TimeSync Does TimeSync automatically synchronizes the time and timezone of every connected device across all your sites. It pushes the correct settings continuously keeping every camera, alarm, and log locked to one verified timeline, with no manual intervention needed.
Why It Matters Mismatched timestamps are more than an inconvenience. They create confusing timelines, cause teams to miss critical details, and produce records that don't hold up when it counts. In a compliance audit or security investigation, even a few seconds of drift can turn a straightforward review into hours of extra work, or worse. TimeSync eliminates that risk. It delivers 100% device synchronization, cuts investigation time by 80%, and enables teams to monitor 200+ additional sites without adding staff. For business leaders, accurate and legally defensible records translate directly to cost savings, anywhere from €10k to €100k annually through avoided legal exposure.
How It Works TimeSync connects to all devices managed within GCXONE and automatically pushes the correct time and timezone settings. The sync runs continuously, so devices stay accurate over time without anyone having to lift a finger. _Figure 1: Time Zone Icon_
Key Capabilities
100% Device Synchronization: Eliminates timestamp mismatches across all sites, so your timelines are always clean and consistent
Automatic Timezone Management: Applies the correct timezone per site and per device, with no manual configuration needed.
Trusted Evidence: Every frame carries an accurate, legally defensible timestamp
Seamless Sync at Scale: Works across thousands of devices simultaneously without performance issues
Zero Drift: Once set up, the system maintains accuracy on its own, with no IT intervention required.
Real-World Use Cases
A security team investigates an incident across multiple camera feeds from different sites. Because TimeSync keeps all devices aligned, they reconstruct the full timeline in minutes instead of hours.
An organization undergoes a compliance audit. TimeSync ensures all recordings carry accurate, verifiable timestamps - creating tamper-proof records that hold up without dispute.
A device restarts after a power outage and its clock resets. TimeSync automatically re-syncs it when it comes back online, preventing any gaps in the recording timeline.
A manager oversees operations across sites in different timezones. TimeSync ensures each device shows the correct local time, preventing cross-site confusion during investigations.
Additional Details Automatic time synchronization is currently supported for HikVision and Dahua devices only. :::note Limited Device Support For all other device types, the Timezone field is still available for manual configuration. However, automatic time synchronization via TimeSync is not supported outside of HikVision and Dahua. ::: _Figure 2: Time Zone Sync for HikVision and Dahua devices_

title: Event Clips Overview
excerpt: What Event Clips Does Event Clips (also known as Alarm Clips) automatically capture short video segments whenever an alarm or motion event is triggered. Instead of just receiving an alert, operators can immediately see what actually happened before t
content:
What Event Clips Does Event Clips (also known as Alarm Clips) automatically capture short video segments whenever an alarm or motion event is triggered. Instead of just receiving an alert, operators can immediately see what actually happened before the event, at the moment of the alarm, and after it, without needing to search through recorded footage. _Figure 1 : Event clip attached to an alarm, showing pre-event, event moment, and post-event context in a single unified view_
Why It Matters When an alarm is triggered, speed and clarity are critical. Without Event Clips, operators typically have to manually review recordings to understand the situation. This slows down response time and adds unnecessary effort. Event Clips remove that friction by attaching video directly to the alarm. This allows operators to quickly verify events, reduce false alarms, and respond with confidence.
How It Works Event Clips rely on a buffer-based recording approach. Devices continuously maintain a short recording buffer. When an alarm is triggered, the system extracts a video segment that includes both pre- and post-event footage. The event clip is attached to the alarm and presented using synchronized views that show pre-event, event moment, and post-event context. If AI is enabled, the clip can also be analyzed and enriched with detection results before being presented to the operator.
image _Figure 2: Event clips are presented using synchronized views, allowing operators to understand what happened before, at the moment of the event, and after it_ Operators can also switch between live view and playback, allowing them to compare real-time activity with recorded event clips. _Figure 3: Playback view of an event clip, enabling operators to navigate through pre-event, event, and post-event footage_
Key Capabilities
Automatic Clip Capture – Clips are generated automatically when an alarm is triggered
Pre- and Post-Event Context – Each clip includes footage from before and after the event
AI Integration – Clips can be analyzed to help classify events and reduce false alarms
Instant Access – Clips are attached directly to alarms for immediate review
Configurable Triggers – Clip capture can be limited to specific alarm types
Real-World Use Cases
An intrusion alarm is triggered, and the operator can instantly see what happened without searching through recordings
A false alarm is quickly dismissed after reviewing the attached clip
Operators process alarm queues faster because each alarm already includes visual context
AI analyzes clips in advance, helping confirm whether an event is real before the operator even opens it
Best Practices
Use at least 5 seconds of pre-alarm recording, since events rarely start exactly at the trigger point.
For better results, consider extending clips to 15 seconds before and after the event (~30 seconds total).
Use continuous recording whenever possible to ensure full context is always available.
Focus clip capture on important alarm types to avoid unnecessary storage usage.
Regularly monitor storage and retention settings, especially in high-alarm environments.
Additional Details
Event Clips depend on device capabilities, including recording support and buffer availability.
Supported devices include ADPRO, Axis, Dahua, Milestone, and Ganz AI BOX.
Some battery-powered devices (such as Ajax or Reconeyez) may provide images instead of video clips due to hardware limitations.
Clip generation can be controlled using device-level configuration, including enabling recording and defining which alarm types should trigger clips.
Typical clip duration is around 10 seconds (5s before + 5s after), but can be adjusted based on site requirements and network conditions.

title: Live View Overview
excerpt:
What Live View Does Live View gives you real-time video monitoring across all integrated cameras in GCXONE. Watch live feeds from any connected site, manage multiple streams simultaneously, and respond to events as they happen.
Why It Matters Delayed
content:
What Live View Does Live View gives you real-time video monitoring across all integrated cameras in GCXONE. Watch live feeds from any connected site, manage multiple streams simultaneously, and respond to events as they happen.
Why It Matters Delayed visibility costs operators critical response time. Live View eliminates that gap by delivering immediate, uninterrupted streams to the Video Viewer. You see what is happening and act before it escalates.
How It Works Open the Device Explorer from the left panel to browse your connected camera tree. Cameras are organized by customer, site, and device. Find and launch any camera feed directly from the device tree. Drag a sensor onto the grid or double-click it in the device tree. Streaming starts immediately. Stream controls are available on each tile. Refresh, close, freeze, or resume any feed instantly. _Figure 1: Device Explorer and Live Feed - Shows the Device Explorer tree with grouped camera devices alongside an active fisheye stream and the full toolbar, giving operators fast access to any camera across all integrated sites._
Key Capabilities Live View gives you full control over how video is displayed, navigated, and interacted with in real time.
Flexible Grid Layouts - Switch between 1x1, 2x2, 3x3, and 4x4 layouts instantly. Adjust layouts without interrupting active streams.
Stream Controls - Refresh, freeze, close, or resume any feed directly from the tile. Access Jump Back, Playback, and Share controls in the expanded single-camera view.
PTZ Controls - Pan, tilt, zoom, and reposition compatible cameras using on-screen controls. Call presets to return to predefined positions. Milestone devices support advanced PTZ modes such as Rectangle PTZ and Point to Center.
Event Review (4-Panel View) - View Live, Pre, Post, and Preview feeds side by side. Review full event context in a single screen.
Sensor Labels - Display sensor names directly on the video stream for quick identification.
Sensor Isolation and Privacy Masks - Isolate a sensor for a defined duration during active incidents. Apply privacy masks to permanently hide sensitive areas. _Figure 2: Multi-Camera Grid View - Shows six active camera feeds displayed simultaneously with labeled tiles, giving operators a complete overview of all monitored zones at once._ _Figure 3: Video Viewer - Single Camera Focus - Shows the Video Viewer with a single expanded feed and the bottom toolbar for Jump Back, Playback, Share, and Freeze/Play controls, enabling focused review with full playback access._ _Figure 4: Event Review - 4-Panel View - Shows Live, Pre, Post, and Preview panes displayed together during an alarm session, enabling side-by-side comparison of real-time and recorded footage._
Real-World Use Cases
An alarm triggers at a remote site. The operator opens Live View and confirms whether a person is present within seconds. AI bounding boxes provide additional context directly in the stream.
Movement is detected at a perimeter camera. The operator uses PTZ controls to track the subject and then restores the camera to its preset position.
A supervisor monitors multiple sites simultaneously using a grid layout, keeping full visibility across locations.
An alarm is assigned in Talos CMS. Live View automatically opens the relevant camera, giving the operator immediate visual context. _Figure 5: Fullscreen Camera Feed - Shows an individual camera in Fullscreen mode with Live and quality indicators, providing an unobstructed view for high-priority incident monitoring._
Best Practices
Organize camera groups in the Device Explorer by zone or priority. This reduces search time during active incidents.
Use PTZ presets after repositioning. Cameras left off-position reduce coverage and create blind spots.
Apply privacy masks during initial configuration. This ensures compliance before capturing sensitive areas.
Train operators on the 4-panel event view. Reviewing multiple timelines together improves decision accuracy.
Additional Details Live View compatibility depends on device type, deployment mode, and streaming protocol. Full support in Cloud and Local modes: Adpro, Axis, Axxon, Dahua, Hikvision, Milestone. Cloud-only support: Hanwha, NetVue, Viasys. No live support: Reconeyez PIR Cams and Ajax PIR cameras (event-based only). Streaming protocols:
RTSP - Axis, Axxon, Hanwha, Hikvision.
MJPEG - Viasys.
TCP JPEG / Raw - Milestone. The operator account must have Live View or Operator permissions enabled on the source device. Required network ports must be open and whitelisted: RTSP (554) and HTTP (80/443). For Local Mode features such as P2P streaming, encrypted streams, and local audio, the Genesis Local Mode component must be installed on the operator workstation.

title: Live View Troubleshooting
excerpt:
Live View Troubleshooting This guide helps you quickly identify and resolve common Live View issues in GCXONE.
Streams Not Starting You open a camera and get a loading spinner, a black screen, or an "Unable to start stream" error. This usually indica
content:
Live View Troubleshooting This guide helps you quickly identify and resolve common Live View issues in GCXONE.
Streams Not Starting You open a camera and get a loading spinner, a black screen, or an "Unable to start stream" error. This usually indicates a connectivity, configuration, or permissions issue.
Verify the device is online in the Device Explorer
Check that the streaming configuration is correct for the device
Confirm the user account has Live View or Operator permissions enabled
Test network connectivity and confirm required ports are open: RTSP (554) and HTTP (80/443)
Poor Stream Quality Video is pixelated, blurry, or lower resolution than expected. This is typically caused by bandwidth limits, stream selection, or device configuration.
Increase the stream quality setting in the viewer
Check available bandwidth - insufficient bandwidth degrades stream quality automatically
Confirm you are using the primary stream, not the sub-stream
Enable Local Mode for workstations on the same network as the device
Layout Issues Layouts are not displaying correctly, cameras appear in wrong positions, or changes are not saving. Layout issues are typically caused by configuration conflicts or saved state inconsistencies. If cameras are missing or misplaced:
Verify cameras are assigned to the correct layout positions
Check that the grid size matches the number of cameras in the layout
Remove unavailable cameras and replace them with active ones
Recreate the layout if it appears corrupted If the layout is not saving:
Confirm the user has layout management permissions
Check if a layout with the same name already exists
Try saving with a different name
Clear browser cache and reload, then save again
Performance Issues Stream startup is slow, video is choppy, or system performance drops when multiple streams are open. This occurs when stream load or system resources exceed available capacity.
Reduce the number of simultaneous streams
Switch to a smaller grid size - use 2x2 instead of 4x4 where possible
Use secondary streams for grid views and reserve primary streams for focused review
Close unused streams when not actively monitoring
Enable Local Mode for on-site workstations to reduce cloud relay load
Check system resource usage - high CPU or memory usage directly impacts stream performance
PTZ Controls Not Available PTZ buttons are missing, disabled, or not responding in Live View. This is usually a device limitation, a permissions gap, or a configuration issue.
Verify the camera supports PTZ - not all devices do
Confirm PTZ is enabled on the device in GCXONE settings
Check that the user account has PTZ permissions
Verify PTZ controls are enabled in Live View settings
Still Having Issues? If the steps above did not resolve the problem, follow these steps:
Check the Video Streaming Troubleshooting guide - many Live View issues are streaming-related
Review GCXONE and device logs for error details
Collect device type, error message, network configuration, and steps already tried before reaching out
Submit a support ticket through the GCXONE support portal

title: Playback Overview
excerpt: What Playback Does Playback allows operators to access, review, and navigate recorded video from connected security devices. It provides the ability to move through past events, analyze incidents, and retrieve video evidence without manually searchin
content:
What Playback Does Playback allows operators to access, review, and navigate recorded video from connected security devices. It provides the ability to move through past events, analyze incidents, and retrieve video evidence without manually searching through long recordings.
Why It Matters Playback is essential for understanding what happened during a security event. It allows operators to:
Investigate incidents in detail
Verify alarms with accurate visual evidence
Reconstruct events across time Without playback, operators are limited to live monitoring and lose access to critical historical context.
How It Works Playback retrieves recorded video from integrated devices or storage systems and presents it through a unified interface. Operators can:
Select a device or camera
Choose a specific time or event
Navigate through recordings using a timeline Playback includes tools for precise control, allowing users to replay, fast-forward, or analyze video in detail.
Key Capabilities Playback provides a set of tools designed to make video investigation faster and more efficient. Timeline Navigation The unified timeline combines recordings from multiple cameras into a single synchronized view. This allows operators to track events across devices without switching between streams. _Figure 1: Unified Timeline Interface - Shows synchronized playback with event markers for fast navigation and investigation._ Multi-Camera Playback Playback supports synchronized viewing across multiple cameras.
Synchronized Playback – View multiple cameras at the same point in time
Grid Views – Monitor several camera feeds simultaneously
Independent Controls – Control each camera stream separately if needed
Comparison Mode – Compare different camera views side-by-side _Figure 2: Multi-Camera Playback View - Multiple synchronized camera feeds displayed for full situational awareness_ Search and Navigation Search and navigation tools help you find the exact moment you need.
Time Search – Jump directly to a specific date and time
Event Search – Find moments based on alarms or motion events
Calendar Navigation – Select dates to access recordings
Bookmarks – Save important timestamps for quick access later _Figure 3: Time Selection and Navigation - Calendar and timeline controls used to locate specific moments in playback._ Playback Controls Playback controls give operators precise control over how video is reviewed. They allow you to play, pause, rewind, fast-forward, and navigate through video with accuracy. Video Export Playback allows you to extract and share important video segments.
Export selected clips in different formats
Choose video quality and resolution
Export multiple clips when needed
Download locally or store in the cloud Recording Sources Playback supports multiple recording sources depending on deployment. Real-World Use Cases
Review footage before and after an alarm to understand the full sequence of events.
Investigate incidents across multiple cameras using synchronized playback.
Quickly locate specific moments using timeline or event search.
Export video clips for reporting or compliance purposes.
Best Practices
Use event markers to jump directly to important moments
Limit the time range to reduce search time
Use multi-camera playback for complex investigations
Export clips with buffer time before and after events
Close unused streams to maintain performance
Additional Details Playback supports multiple recording sources, including cameras and NVRs, VMS platforms, cloud storage, and hybrid deployments. Playback performance depends on network bandwidth and system configuration. Storage availability determines how far back recordings can be accessed, and recording gaps may limit playback availability.
