excerpt: What Playback Does Playback allows operators to access, review, and navigate recorded video from connected security devices. It provides the ability to move through past events, analyze incidents, and retrieve video evidence without manually searchin
content:
What Playback Does Playback allows operators to access, review, and navigate recorded video from connected security devices. It provides the ability to move through past events, analyze incidents, and retrieve video evidence without manually searching through long recordings.
Why It Matters Playback is essential for understanding what happened during a security event. It allows operators to:
Investigate incidents in detail
Verify alarms with accurate visual evidence
Reconstruct events across time Without playback, operators are limited to live monitoring and lose access to critical historical context.
How It Works Playback retrieves recorded video from integrated devices or storage systems and presents it through a unified interface. Operators can:
Select a device or camera
Choose a specific time or event
Navigate through recordings using a timeline Playback includes tools for precise control, allowing users to replay, fast-forward, or analyze video in detail.
Key Capabilities
Timeline Navigation Playback provides a chronological timeline of recorded video, allowing operators to scroll through history and quickly locate specific moments. Event markers (such as motion or alarms) help highlight important points in time.
Playback Controls Operators can control playback using standard tools such as play, pause, rewind, fast-forward, and frame-by-frame navigation for detailed analysis.
Smart Playback Smart Playback optimizes video review by automatically detecting motion. It plays segments with activity at normal speed while skipping idle periods, significantly reducing review time.
Search and Filtering Playback supports multiple ways to locate footage:
Search by date and time
Search by event type (e.g., motion or alarms)
Navigate using logs or recorded activity
Video Tagging Operators can tag specific moments during playback (such as people or locations), making it easier to find important footage later.
Real-World Use Cases
Reviewing recorded footage after an alarm to understand what happened
Investigating incidents across multiple time periods
Quickly locating motion events using smart playback
Tagging important moments for future reference or reporting
Best Practices
Ensure devices are time-synchronized to avoid incorrect playback results
Use Smart Playback to reduce investigation time
Focus on event-based navigation instead of scanning full recordings
Verify recording settings and retention policies
Additional Details
Device Compatibility Playback support depends on the device and integration type.
Supported devices: ADPRO, Hikvision, Axis, Dahua, Milestone, Senstar, Uniview
Limited support:
Viasys (Cloud Mode)
Hanwha (event-based video lookup)
Ganz AI BOX (Local Mode only)
Not supported:
Ajax PIR-CAMs and Reconeyez (image-based only)
Camect (no continuous playback support)
Technical Requirements
Time Synchronization Device time must match system time to ensure correct playback results (NTP recommended)
User Permissions Playback and live view permissions must be enabled for user roles
Local vs Cloud Mode Local Mode is preferred for advanced playback features such as multi-camera playback and video export

title: PTZ Control Configuration
excerpt:
PTZ Control Configuration PTZ Control Configuration defines how PTZ cameras are set up and managed in GCXONE to ensure reliable control, accurate positioning, and consistent performance. Estimated time: 20-30 minutes per PTZ camera.
Why It Matters In
content:
PTZ Control Configuration PTZ Control Configuration defines how PTZ cameras are set up and managed in GCXONE to ensure reliable control, accurate positioning, and consistent performance. Estimated time: 20-30 minutes per PTZ camera.
Why It Matters Incorrect PTZ configuration prevents camera movement, limits control, and delays response during live monitoring. When an operator needs to move a camera fast, a misconfigured preset or missing permission is the last thing they should be dealing with. Proper setup ensures operators can control cameras instantly and use features like presets and tours without interruptions.
How It Works PTZ configuration connects device-level settings with GCXONE controls. The device provides PTZ capabilities, while GCXONE manages control behavior, presets, and automation. Once configured, operators can control cameras in real time and trigger predefined movements instantly.
Key Capabilities
Device Integration - Enable PTZ functionality and ensure compatibility with supported protocols.
Platform Control - Configure PTZ behavior directly within GCXONE.
Presets - Save and recall predefined camera positions.
Preset Tours - Automatically move between multiple preset positions.
Automation - Trigger PTZ movement based on alarms or events.
Home Position - Define a default camera position the camera returns to after inactivity.
Prerequisites Before starting, confirm the following:
PTZ camera is successfully added to GCXONE.
Camera hardware supports PTZ functionality.
Device is online and accessible.
Video streaming is working correctly.
Appropriate user permissions are available.
Configuration Steps
Step 1 - Device-Level Setup Access the camera web interface and navigate to PTZ Settings or Camera Control.
Enable PTZ and select the appropriate protocol such as ONVIF or manufacturer-specific.
Verify the PTZ address - typically 1 or auto-detect.
Set movement limits, idle time, and home position.
Click Save. Next, verify protocol settings under Network or Protocol Settings.
Confirm ONVIF PTZ is enabled and verify the control port.
Ensure the integration user has PTZ permissions on the device.
Test pan, tilt, zoom, and preset recall from the device interface. Note: Exact navigation varies by manufacturer. Refer to device documentation for specific steps.
Step 2 - GCXONE Platform Setup Navigate to Devices, select the PTZ camera, click Camera Configuration, and go to the PTZ Settings tab.
Enable PTZ Control, select the protocol, and click Test PTZ Connection.
Set default speed from 1-8 and enable Invert Controls if needed.
Configure Control Timeout to prevent conflicts between users.
Enable Preset Support and Tour Support if applicable.
Click Save Configuration.
Step 3 - User Permissions Navigate to Settings, User Management, and select the user or role.
Enable View PTZ Controls, Control PTZ, Manage Presets, and Control Tours as needed.
Restrict to specific cameras or time windows if required.
Click Save Permissions.
Step 4 - Preset Configuration In Live View, select the PTZ camera and open the PTZ Controls panel.
Position the camera using pan, tilt, and zoom controls.
Click Save Preset, enter a descriptive name such as "Front Entrance" or "Parking Lot North", and assign a preset number from 1-255.
Click Save and verify the preset recalls correctly. To manage existing presets, navigate to Devices, select the camera, go to PTZ Settings, then Preset Management to edit, reorder, delete, or export presets.
Step 5 - Preset Tours Navigate to Devices, select the camera, go to PTZ Settings, then Tours.
Click Create Tour and enter a name such as "Perimeter Scan".
Enable Repeat for continuous tours.
Add presets, set dwell time at each position, and set movement speed between presets.
Configure start position and end behavior, then click Save. To automate tours, navigate to Settings, Automation, then PTZ Tours to set schedules or event-based triggers.
Step 6 - Automation Rules Navigate to Settings, Automation, then PTZ Rules.
Click Create Rule and select the trigger and target camera.
Configure the PTZ action: move to preset, start tour, or specific movement.
Set delay before action, duration at preset, and return behavior.
Click Save Rule. Example: When motion is detected at the Front Door sensor, the PTZ camera moves to Preset 1 (Front Entrance) for 60 seconds, then returns to home position.
Real-World Use Cases
An operator configures presets for key areas such as entrances and critical zones for one-click positioning during incidents.
An alarm triggers and the camera automatically moves to a predefined preset without any manual input.
A supervisor sets up tours to monitor large areas without relying on manual control throughout the shift.
A team standardizes preset layouts across multiple cameras so every operator works from the same reference points.
Best Practices
Use descriptive preset names - "Front Entrance" is faster to act on than "Preset 1" during a live incident.
Number critical presets sequenti

title: PTZ Control Overview
excerpt: What PTZ Control Does PTZ (Pan-Tilt-Zoom) Control allows operators to remotely adjust camera position, angle, and zoom level in real time from the GCXONE interface. This enables active monitoring, faster incident response, and precise control over ca
content:
What PTZ Control Does PTZ (Pan-Tilt-Zoom) Control allows operators to remotely adjust camera position, angle, and zoom level in real time from the GCXONE interface. This enables active monitoring, faster incident response, and precise control over camera coverage.
Why It Matters Fixed cameras provide a limited view. When events occur outside that view, operators lose visibility. PTZ control lets operators dynamically adjust coverage, follow movement, and focus on critical details in real time, improving situational awareness and reducing the need for additional cameras.
How It Works The operator sends a command through the GCXONE interface. GCXONE translates the command into the correct device protocol and sends it to the camera. The camera executes the movement and updates its position in real time.
Key Capabilities PTZ control provides a complete set of camera movement and control capabilities.
Pan and Tilt - Move the camera horizontally and vertically to adjust the viewing angle
Zoom - Adjust optical or digital zoom to focus on specific areas
Focus and Iris - Control image sharpness and light exposure on supported devices
Presets - Save and instantly return to predefined camera positions
Preset Tours - Automatically move between preset positions
Pattern Recording - Record and replay movement sequences
Speed Control - Adjust movement speed for precision or rapid scanning
Relative Movement - Move the camera in small increments
Absolute Positioning - Move the camera to exact coordinates on supported devices
Advanced Controls (Milestone) - Use Rectangle PTZ or Point to Center for faster navigation
Control Methods - Use on-screen controls, keyboard shortcuts, mobile gestures, or preset selection
Real-World Use Cases
An operator tracks movement across a large area in real time using pan and tilt
An operator moves the camera to the event location immediately when an alarm triggers
An operator zooms in on details and captures different angles for evidence
A supervisor monitors multiple zones using presets and tours without manual repositioning
Best Practices
Use descriptive preset names like "Front Entrance" or "Gate 1" - Generic names slow operators down during incidents
Use presets instead of manual movement for faster, more consistent positioning
Adjust speed based on the situation - fast for scanning, slow for precision work
Avoid excessive manual movement to reduce mechanical wear on the camera
Verify preset accuracy regularly - cameras can drift over time
Additional Details PTZ functionality depends on device capabilities and configuration.
Supported Devices - Available on cameras with PTZ hardware and protocol support (ONVIF or manufacturer integration)
Permissions - Users must have PTZ control permissions enabled on their account
Physical Limits - Cameras have defined pan and tilt ranges depending on the model
Network Impact - Latency affects responsiveness, especially in remote environments. Local Mode reduces this significantly
Usage Constraints - Only one operator should control a PTZ camera at a time to avoid conflicting commands

title: Video Streaming Configuration
excerpt:
Video Streaming Configuration This guide explains how to configure video streaming in GCXONE for stable performance across devices and environments.
Why It Matters Incorrect streaming configuration leads to delays, buffering, or failed connections. G
content:
Video Streaming Configuration This guide explains how to configure video streaming in GCXONE for stable performance across devices and environments.
Why It Matters Incorrect streaming configuration leads to delays, buffering, or failed connections. Getting this right ensures reliable video delivery and a consistent experience for every operator.
Configuration Overview Streaming configuration covers four areas:
Device-level setup.
GCXONE platform configuration.
Optional Local Mode setup.
Performance optimization and verification.
Network Requirements Streaming requires reliable network connectivity and proper access configuration.
Ensure devices are reachable from GCXONE.
Open required ports: RTSP (554) and HTTP/HTTPS (80/443).
Configure firewall rules to allow communication between devices and the platform.
Streaming Modes GCXONE supports two streaming modes depending on the environment.
Cloud Mode - Recommended for remote access and standard deployments.
Local Mode - Recommended for on-site monitoring and low-latency performance. Local Mode requires installation on operator workstations and automatically falls back to cloud streaming if unavailable.
Device Configuration Different devices may require specific settings to enable streaming.
Enable required streaming protocols on the device.
Configure user permissions for video access.
Ensure required ports are open and accessible.
Adjust device settings based on manufacturer guidelines. Refer to your device documentation for detailed configuration steps.
Optimization Streaming performance depends on how the deployment is configured. A few adjustments go a long way.
Use secondary streams for multi-camera views.
Enable adaptive streaming (GreenStream) to reduce bandwidth usage automatically.
Avoid excessive bitrate settings on devices - higher is not always better.
Use Local Mode for on-site environments to reduce latency and cloud relay load.
Integration Requirements Some features require additional setup before they work with streaming.
Enable Auto Streaming for alarm workflows.
Ensure required integrations are active in GCXONE.
Keep operator applications and tools properly configured.
Verification After configuration, confirm streaming is working as expected before going live.
Live video loads without delay.
Streams start within a few seconds of opening a feed.
Video quality is stable with no resolution drops.
No buffering or connection drops occur during extended viewing.

title: Video Streaming Overview
excerpt:
Video Streaming Overview Video Streaming delivers real-time video from connected cameras and devices across GCXONE. Operators access live feeds from any location with consistent performance, low latency, and high visual clarity.
Why It Matters Delays
content:
Video Streaming Overview Video Streaming delivers real-time video from connected cameras and devices across GCXONE. Operators access live feeds from any location with consistent performance, low latency, and high visual clarity.
Why It Matters Delays, poor quality, or dropped connections directly impact decision-making and operational efficiency. Reliable streaming is critical for real-time monitoring. GCXONE delivers stable, low-latency streaming across both local and remote environments. Operators always have the visual context they need to respond effectively.
How It Works Operators open a camera feed from the GCXONE interface. GCXONE connects to the device using the appropriate streaming protocol and delivers the stream directly to the operator. Streaming adapts automatically based on network conditions, device capabilities, and the number of active streams.
Key Capabilities GCXONE provides flexible streaming capabilities designed for performance, compatibility, and scalability.
Multi-Protocol Support - Supports RTSP, HLS, SDK integrations, and other streaming methods depending on the device
Cloud Streaming - Access video from anywhere without additional setup
Local Mode (P2P) - Establish direct connections for near-zero latency on local networks
Adaptive Streaming (GreenStream) - Automatically adjusts video quality based on viewport size and system load
Low Bandwidth Mode - Maintains usable video in constrained network environments
Streaming Modes GCXONE supports two primary streaming modes based on the network environment and deployment setup.
Cloud Streaming - Routes video through GCXONE infrastructure. Provides remote access, encryption, and simplified setup
Local Mode - Creates a direct connection between the operator and the device. Reduces latency and bandwidth usage Local Mode requires installation on the operator workstation and automatically falls back to cloud streaming if unavailable.
Stream Quality Management GreenStream automatically adjusts video resolution based on how the feed is displayed.
Auto - Recommended. Adjusts quality dynamically per stream
High - Full resolution for detailed review
Medium - Balanced quality for multi-camera views
Low - Optimized for limited bandwidth This ensures efficient use of bandwidth and system resources without manual tuning.
Supported Devices GCXONE supports a wide range of devices and platforms.
IP Cameras - Axis, Hikvision, Dahua, Hanwha, and ONVIF-compatible devices
NVRs and DVRs - Supported through RTSP or native integrations
VMS Platforms - Milestone, Avigilon, and others
Specialized Devices - Camect, Reconeyez, and edge-processing systems
Real-World Use Cases
An operator monitors multiple cameras across sites using live feeds and grid layouts
An operator opens video instantly when an alarm is triggered to verify the incident
A supervisor accesses footage securely from a remote location without additional setup
Multiple operators view the same stream simultaneously without impacting performance
Deployment Considerations Streaming performance depends on network conditions and system configuration.
Bandwidth - 2 Mbps per stream minimum, higher for HD video
Latency - Lower latency improves real-time monitoring accuracy
Ports - RTSP (554), HTTP/HTTPS (80/443), plus device-specific ports
Firewall - Ensure required ports are open and accessible
Best Practices
Use Auto (GreenStream) mode as the default for all operators
Limit the number of concurrent streams to reduce system load
Use Local Mode for on-site monitoring environments
Configure reasonable bitrate settings on devices
Use secure connections and strong credentials for all devices

title: Video Streaming Troubleshooting
excerpt:
Video Streaming Troubleshooting This guide helps you quickly identify and resolve common video streaming issues in GCXONE.
No Video Display Video does not load, shows a black screen, or displays a "No video available" message. This usually points to
content:
Video Streaming Troubleshooting This guide helps you quickly identify and resolve common video streaming issues in GCXONE.
No Video Display Video does not load, shows a black screen, or displays a "No video available" message. This usually points to a connectivity, credentials, or codec issue.
Verify the device is online and reachable from GCXONE.
Check device credentials in GCXONE - incorrect credentials block the stream entirely.
Confirm RTSP port (554) is open and accessible.
Verify the correct stream path is configured for the device.
Ensure the device uses a supported codec (H.264 or H.265).
Poor Video Quality Video appears blurry, pixelated, or choppy. In most cases this comes down to bitrate, bandwidth, or stream selection.
Increase bitrate on the device if the network can support it.
Reduce stream quality in GCXONE if bandwidth is limited.
Use secondary streams for multi-camera layouts - primary streams are better reserved for focused review.
Enable adaptive quality (GreenStream) to let the platform adjust automatically.
Check available network bandwidth - sustained drops degrade quality without warning.
Buffering or Stuttering Video pauses frequently or playback is unstable. This is almost always a load or network stability problem.
Reduce the number of active streams.
Close unused video panels.
Check network stability and packet loss - even small amounts of packet loss cause stuttering.
Lower device bitrate if the current setting exceeds what the network can handle.
Verify device performance and resource usage.
Connection Drops Video disconnects or stops unexpectedly. Firewall timeouts and network instability are the most common causes.
Check network stability between the operator and the device.
Verify firewall and timeout settings - overly aggressive timeouts drop connections during low-activity periods.
Increase streaming timeout values in GCXONE settings.
Reduce concurrent connections to the device if it is at capacity.
Restart the device if drops persist after other checks.
Local Mode Issues Local Mode is enabled but not working as expected.
Verify the Local Mode service is running on the operator workstation.
Ensure the workstation and device are on the same local network.
Confirm the device supports Local Mode.
Check network routing and firewall rules between the workstation and device.
Restart the Local Mode service if the issue persists after other checks.
Slow Stream Startup Video takes too long to load after opening a feed.
Use Local Mode for on-site access - it significantly reduces startup time.
Check network latency between the operator and the device.
Verify the correct stream configuration is set for the device.
Reduce device load and bitrate if the device is under heavy processing load.
Update device firmware - outdated firmware can cause startup delays.
Audio Issues No audio, poor quality, or sync problems with the video feed.
Enable audio on the device and confirm it is enabled in GCXONE.
Verify the device uses a supported audio codec.
Adjust buffer settings if audio is out of sync with video.
Use Local Mode for better audio performance on on-site workstations.
Still Having Issues? If the steps above did not resolve the problem, here is what to do next:
Review device and GCXONE logs for error details.
Collect device details, network configuration, and any error messages before reaching out.
Submit a support ticket through the GCXONE support portal with the collected information.

title: ZenMode
excerpt: What ZenMode Does ZenMode is an intelligent operator-assist mode built into GCXONE that helps security teams cut through alert noise and focus on what actually matters. Instead of letting operators drown in hundreds of alarms per shift, ZenMode filte
content:
What ZenMode Does ZenMode is an intelligent operator-assist mode built into GCXONE that helps security teams cut through alert noise and focus on what actually matters. Instead of letting operators drown in hundreds of alarms per shift, ZenMode filters, prioritizes, and presents alerts in a way that keeps people sharp — without overwhelming them. The table below highlights the real-world impact of ZenMode on operator performance and efficiency. Why It Matters Every operator knows this feeling. After about an hour into a shift, the alerts keep coming non-stop. Over time, it becomes hard to stay focused, and when something important happens, your attention isn’t as sharp as it should be. This isn’t a people problem, it’s a system problem. That’s exactly what ZenMode is designed to fix. ZenMode doesn’t replace human judgment, it supports it. By filtering out up to 60% of low-priority alarms, it helps operators focus only on what really matters. This leads to faster response times, with each alarm handled in 2–3 minutes. It also allows one operator to manage a large site, even more than 200 cameras per shift, without feeling overwhelmed or losing focus. ****
How It Works Prerequisites: Before you switch ZenMode on, make sure these are in place:
Administrator access to your GCXONE tenant
Permissions to activate Marketplace features
Historical alarm data and recorded footage within the configured retention period — the more history available, the more useful ZenMode becomes Activating ZenMode: ZenMode is activated through the GCXONE Marketplace. Follow these steps:
Log in to GCXONE as an administrator.
Open Marketplace from the main menu.
Select Apps.
Find ZenMode and click Explore. Review the feature details. _Figure 1: ZenMode in Marketplace - Explore page with feature details and activation option._
Click Proceed.
When prompted to save the subscription, click Yes. After subscribing, ZenMode will appear under My Subscriptions and is immediately available for operators to use. Using ZenMode via Video Search:
Navigate to Video Search in GCXONE.
Use the filters at the top to choose a customer or site.
Click the ZenMode icon in the top-right toolbar. _Figure 2: The ZenMode icon in Video Search toolbar._
Select the alarm type or priority you want to focus on. _Figure 3: ZenMode Additional filters_
ZenMode compiles the relevant events into a reviewable video. _Figure 4: ZenMode event compilation view._
Use the playback controls to move through the footage. _Figure 5: ZenMode playback controls interface._ **** Using ZenMode via Video Viewer:
Open Two Tabs
Open the first tab and navigate to the Video Viewer _Figure 6: Video Viewer Tab._
Open a second tab and navigate to Video Search _Figure 7: Video Search tab_
Enable Multi Monitor
Go to the Video Viewer tab (Tab 1)
At the bottom left, make sure the Multi Monitor toggle is turned ON _Figure 8: Multi Monitor enabled in Video Viewer_
Go to the Video Search tab (Tab 2)
At the top right, make sure the Multi Monitor is enabled _Figure 9: Multi Monitor enabled in Video Search_
Select a Camera from Video Search
In the Video Search tab (Tab 2), browse or search for the customer or site
Click on the desired camera to select it _Figure 10: Camera selection in Video Search_
View the Camera in Video Viewer
The selected camera stream will now appear in the Video Viewer tab (Tab 1)
This quad-view allows operators to assess what happened before, during, and after an alarm without switching between separate views. _Figure 11: Quad-view layout_
Activate ZenMode
In the Video Viewer tab, look at the top right area
Click the ZenMode button to activate it
The view will switch to a focused, distraction-free ZenMode display _Figure 12: ZenMode activated view_
Select a Camera to Inspect
In ZenMode, click on any camera tile to open a detailed popup view
The popup shows the camera name, date, and timestamp of the event
Use the playback controls at the bottom to navigate the footage:
Mask [M] – Apply privacy masks to the video
Jump Back [J] – Go back to a previous moment in the recording
Playback [P] – Play the recorded footage
Share [S] – Share the video clip with others
Live [L] – Switch back to the live camera feed
Pause/Play [C] – Pause or resume playback
Close the popup to return to the full ZenMode grid view
:::note Note Video Search displays events from oldest to newest, while Video Viewer shows events from newest to oldest. ::: ****
Key Capabilities
Noise reduction – Operators focus on real events, not junk alarms
Faster response – Each alarm handled in 2–3 minutes
Live + historical – Review past footage without interrupting live monitoring
Playback controls – Pause, rewind, adjust speed, and jump to specific timestamps
Isolate view – Focus the full screen on one camera, hiding all other feeds
Download clips – Export footage for reports, documentation, or legal use
Audit trail – Clear record of what happened, when, and what action was taken
Threshold configuration – Set filters to match

title: First-Time Login & Access
excerpt: Overview This section outlines the complete process for accessing GCXONE for the first time. It covers account registration, verification, password setup, and the initial login procedure. Each step must be completed in sequence to activate successful
content:
Overview This section outlines the complete process for accessing GCXONE for the first time. It covers account registration, verification, password setup, and the initial login procedure. Each step must be completed in sequence to activate successfully and access the platform. Note: There are two ways to access GCXONE for the first time:
Self-registration (new users create their own account)
Administrator-created accounts (users receive a temporary password) Follow the steps below based on your account setup. Some steps may not apply depending on whether your account was self-registered or created by an administrator.
Account Registration & Activation Alternative Access Method : If your account was created by your administrator, you will receive a temporary password via email instead of registering manually. In this case, proceed directly to the login steps below.
Access the Registration Portal Navigate to the GCXONE Portal (https://gcx.nxgen.cloud/) using a supported web browser (Chrome, Edge, or Firefox). Upon accessing the portal, the following elements are displayed:
A professional login interface
A prominent Register button for new users Select Register to begin the onboarding process. Complete the Sign-Up Process The onboarding follows a guided three-stage wizard: Personal Info → Setup Cameras → Finishing Up Each stage must be completed before proceeding.
Stage 1: Personal Information Enter the following details:
First Name — Account holder's first name
Last Name — Account holder's last name
Email — A valid business email address Select Continue to proceed. Stage 2: Setup Cameras Configure your tenant environment and select a deployment type. Tenant Configuration:
Tenant Name — Enter the official name for the tenant environment
Logo _(Optional)_ — Upload a company logo for branding Camera Selection — choose the option that aligns with your environment: Select Continue to proceed. Stage 3: Finishing Up The final stage summarizes the activation process.
Account Activation — Check your email for activation instructions
Platform Access — Access the dashboard after activation is completed Select Register to finalize the registration. Account Verification & Password Setup
Email Verification After completing registration, a verification email is sent from: support@nxgen.io Open the email and select Confirm my account to activate your account.
️ Ensure emails from nxgen.io are not blocked by spam filters. If the verification email is not received:
Check spam or junk folders
Add nxgen.io to your email whitelist
Verify that corporate firewall policies are not blocking automated emails
Contact your IT department if filtering is suspected
Password Creation After confirming your account, you will be redirected to the secure password setup page hosted by Auth0.
Password Requirements
Minimum 8 characters
At least 3 of the following: lowercase letters, uppercase letters, numbers, special characters (e.g. !@#$%^&*) Re-enter the password to confirm and select Reset password.
Login Process The following steps apply to all users after account setup is completed (either through self-registration or administrator invitation).
Initial Login Return to the GCXONE portal and sign in using your newly created credentials. If you received a temporary password from your administrator, use it to sign in. You will be prompted to create a new password on first login.
:::note Note Each user logs in with assigned credentials. Access is controlled based on role configuration — users only see features and entities relevant to their permissions. :::
Dashboard Overview After successful authentication, you are redirected to the GCXONE Dashboard — the central operational interface of the platform. The dashboard displays:
Total Alarms — overall alarm count across all sites
Alarms Passed — alarms processed and forwarded
Alarms Filtered Out — false alarms suppressed by NOVA99x
Unhealthy Cameras — devices flagged by HealthCheck
Checked Cameras / Healthy Cameras — camera health summary
Connection Failure — devices with connectivity issues
Real vs False Alarms — pie chart showing alarm accuracy ratio Additional interface elements:
Left-side navigation menu for core modules
Global search bar for quick navigation
Top-right controls: language selection, notifications, dark/light mode, and account options
Customer and site filters for scoped views
Time range selector for historical data
The dashboard adapts based on your role — operators see alarm widgets, managers see KPIs, and technicians see device health.
Profile Review After logging in, review your user profile and ensure that your contact information and notification preferences are correctly configured.
Login Troubleshooting If you did not receive a temporary password:
Check your spam or junk folder
Contact your administrator to resend the invitation If your temporary password has expired:
Request a new password from your administrator If your account is locked after multiple failed login attem

Level 3: Site Go to the Sites tab and click Add New. Basic Information

Click Import

Fill in the required data and upload the Excel file Site Synchronization with TALOS By default, GCX-ONE and TALOS are automatically synchronized. When you add a new site, it will appear directly in TALOS. If a specific site is not fully synced, you can manually force synchronization:
Push to TALOS — Sends data from GCX-ONE to TALOS
Pull from TALOS — Retrieves data from TALOS into GCX-ONE You can also arm or disarm a site directly from this view if required.
:::warning To delete a site, you must be at the Customer level. You cannot delete a site from the Site-level view. :::
Level 4: Device A Device is the hardware unit (NVR, DVR, hub, or integration) installed at a site. Each device belongs to a specific Site and Customer. Level 5: Sensor A Sensor is an individual camera or detection point connected to a device. Sensors are the lowest level of the hierarchy and are what GCXONE monitors in real time. Go to Configuration → Sensors and click Add New (or add sensors directly from within a site's Sensors tab). Add Sensor fields:

:::note The Sensors list shows: Sensor name, Date, Device, Site, Device Type, Manufacturer, Zone, Audio, Active status, and Actions. :::

title: Password Management & Security
excerpt:
Password Requirements GCXONE enforces strong password policies to protect your account. All passwords must meet the following minimum requirements:
Minimum 12 characters in length
At least one uppercase letter (A-Z)
At least one lowercase letter (a-z
content:
Password Requirements GCXONE enforces strong password policies to protect your account. All passwords must meet the following minimum requirements:
Minimum 12 characters in length
At least one uppercase letter (A-Z)
At least one lowercase letter (a-z)
At least one number (0-9)
At least one special character (!@#$%^&*)
Changing Your Password To change your password, navigate to your user profile and select "Change Password." Enter your current password, then enter and confirm your new password. Password changes take effect immediately.
Password Expiration Depending on your organization settings, passwords may expire after 90 days. You will receive a notification 14 days before expiration. Update your password before it expires to avoid account lockout.
Two-Factor Authentication Enable two-factor authentication (2FA) for an additional layer of security. GCXONE supports authenticator apps (Google Authenticator, Microsoft Authenticator) and SMS verification.
Password Recovery If you forget your password, use the "Forgot Password" link on the login page. Enter your registered email address to receive a password reset link. The link expires after 24 hours.
Security Best Practices
Never share your password with others
Use a unique password for GCXONE
Consider using a password manager
Report suspicious activity immediately

title: Pre-Deployment Requirements
excerpt:
Overview Before starting the GCXONE setup process, confirm that your network, browser, and device infrastructure meet the requirements below. Completing this checklist in advance prevents delays during onboarding and registration.
Network Requirement
content:
Overview Before starting the GCXONE setup process, confirm that your network, browser, and device infrastructure meet the requirements below. Completing this checklist in advance prevents delays during onboarding and registration.
Network Requirements
Bandwidth A minimum of 10 Mbps is required for standard platform operations. Video streaming and large data exports will require additional bandwidth depending on the number of active cameras and sites.
Required Ports The following ports must be open on your firewall before deployment:
443 (HTTPS/WSS): Required for all web platform access, real-time WebSocket updates, REST API calls, and outbound webhooks. This is the primary port for GCXONE.
80 (HTTP): Used for HTTP-to-HTTPS redirects. Optional but recommended to ensure smooth browser navigation.
161/UDP (SNMP): Required for monitoring SNMP-enabled devices such as network equipment and power systems.
162/UDP (SNMP Trap): Required to receive SNMP trap alerts from monitored devices.
502 (Modbus TCP): Required for integrating industrial equipment and power systems using the Modbus protocol.
1883 / 8883 (MQTT): Required for IoT device connectivity. Port 8883 is the encrypted variant and is preferred.
DNS and Domain Access Your network must be able to resolve and reach the nxgen.cloud domain. If you use internal DNS, add the appropriate A records or CNAME entries before deployment. Confirm this by running a DNS lookup from within your network before proceeding.
Proxy Configuration If your organization routes traffic through a proxy server, ensure the proxy is configured to allow WebSocket connections on port 443. Standard HTTP proxies may block WebSocket traffic, which is required for real-time alarm updates.
Browser Requirements GCXONE runs entirely in the browser. No software installation is required on operator workstations. The following browsers are supported:
Google Chrome 90 or later (recommended)
Microsoft Edge 90 or later
Mozilla Firefox 88 or later
Safari 14 or later JavaScript must be enabled. A minimum screen resolution of 1920x1080 is recommended for full dashboard visibility.
Account Requirements Before your account can be activated, have the following ready:
Corporate email address: Used for registration and receiving your activation email. Personal email addresses are not accepted.
Administrative access: You will need permission to configure firewall rules and network settings during setup.
Primary and secondary contacts: Required for account recovery and escalation. Both contacts should be reachable during the deployment window.
Device and Site Preparation If you plan to add cameras, sensors, or other devices during or immediately after setup, prepare the following in advance:
Device credentials: Administrator usernames and passwords for all cameras and sensors to be integrated.
IP addresses and port numbers: Document the IP address and communication port for each device.
Network topology: Subnet details and gateway addresses for each site where devices will be deployed.
Device firmware: Verify that all devices are running up-to-date firmware before integration to avoid compatibility issues.

title: Required Ports & Network Configuration
excerpt:
Network Requirements Overview GCXONE requires specific network configurations to function properly. This document outlines the ports and protocols needed for platform access, device communication, and third-party integrations.
Web Platform Access
HTT
content:
Network Requirements Overview GCXONE requires specific network configurations to function properly. This document outlines the ports and protocols needed for platform access, device communication, and third-party integrations.
Web Platform Access
HTTPS (443) - Required for secure web access to the platform
HTTP (80) - Redirects to HTTPS, optional but recommended
WebSocket (443) - Used for real-time updates and notifications
Device Communication
SNMP (161/UDP) - For SNMP-enabled monitoring devices
SNMP Trap (162/UDP) - For receiving SNMP trap alerts
Modbus TCP (502) - For Modbus-enabled equipment
MQTT (1883/8883) - For IoT device connectivity
API and Integration
REST API (443) - HTTPS endpoint for API calls
Webhooks (443) - Outbound webhook notifications
Firewall Configuration Configure your firewall to allow outbound connections to the GCXONE platform on ports 443 and 80. For on-premises deployments, ensure the necessary inbound ports are open for device communication.
Network Bandwidth Minimum recommended bandwidth is 10 Mbps for standard operations. Video streaming and large data exports may require additional bandwidth allocation.
DNS Requirements Ensure your DNS servers can resolve the GCXONE platform hostname. If using internal DNS, add the appropriate A records or CNAME entries for your deployment.
Proxy Configuration If your organization uses a proxy server, configure the proxy settings in your browser or system preferences. WebSocket connections may require special proxy configuration to function correctly.

Common Issues and Solutions
Page Not Loading
Clear your browser cache and cookies
Disable browser extensions temporarily
Try an incognito or private browsing window
Check your network connection

Check if cookies are being blocked

Performance Optimization
Close unused browser tabs to free memory
Disable unnecessary browser extensions
Ensure your browser is updated to the latest version
Consider increasing browser cache size

title: User Management Setup
excerpt: Overview GCXONE uses a Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) system to manage user permissions across the platform. Access is defined through roles, module privileges, and entity assignments. info Key Concept Privileges determine WHAT a user can do. Entit
content:
Overview GCXONE uses a Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) system to manage user permissions across the platform. Access is defined through roles, module privileges, and entity assignments. info Key Concept Privileges determine WHAT a user can do. Entity access determines WHICH customers, sites, and devices they can see. These are two separate things.
Review Default Roles GCXONE provides predefined roles to simplify initial configuration: **** These roles can be used directly or customized depending on your operational requirements.
Create or Modify Roles Navigate to Settings → Roles → Configure New Role The role creation wizard walks you through 4 steps:
Role Info — Name, description, and optional default role toggle
Users — Select which users belong to this role
Privileges — Enable or disable access per module (Dashboard, Video Viewer, Alarm Manager, Map, and more)
Entity Assignment — Choose which customers, sites, and devices users of this role can access Enable Include Children when selecting entities so any new devices or sensors added later are automatically accessible — no manual updates needed.
Invite Users Navigate to Settings → Users and invite users by email. During the invitation process:
A role is assigned to the user
Module privileges are inherited from the assigned role
Entity access is applied based on role configuration After invitation, users receive an email to activate their account.
For further information, visit the Roles and Permissions page.

title: What is GCXOne
excerpt: Overview GCXONE is a cloud-based, AI-powered monitoring and operations platform that delivers unified video surveillance and IoT services as a Security Management SaaS solution. It enables centralized, remote monitoring without requiring local infras
content:
Overview GCXONE is a cloud-based, AI-powered monitoring and operations platform that delivers unified video surveillance and IoT services as a Security Management SaaS solution. It enables centralized, remote monitoring without requiring local infrastructure, providing a single intelligent interface for alarms, dashboards, device health, workflows, towers, and AI tools. GCXONE is built as an operational control center designed for efficiency, scalability, and growth. It goes beyond traditional monitoring.
What Problem Does GCXONE Solve? Security operations centers and service providers typically face:
High volumes of false alarms from cameras triggered by trees, headlights, animals, and weather, causing alarm fatigue
Slow, manual investigations and fragmented workflows
Cameras going offline, obstructed, or degraded without anyone noticing
Complex onboarding of new customers, sites, and devices at scale
Limited visibility across distributed locations and stakeholders GCXONE addresses these challenges by placing AI at the core of every step in the monitoring workflow.
How GCXONE Works Every alarm in GCXONE follows a structured lifecycle:
Devices, cameras, and sensors send events to GCXONE
NOVA99x AI filters up to 99% of false alarms in real time using computer vision
Verified alarms are delivered to operators in Talos with full context: GIF preview, site info, device reference, and alarm classification
Operators review, confirm, or escalate. Automated workflows then handle notification, dispatch, or SLA reporting
Every alarm is closed with a resolution note and logged in the Audit Log for compliance and reporting This creates a traceable, intelligent operational lifecycle from event detection to resolution.
Core Operational Value
Reduce Alarm Fatigue with NOVA99x NOVA99x is GCXONE's intelligent false-alarm filtering module. It analyzes every motion-triggered event in real time using computer vision, running three core algorithms: Advanced Object Detection, Dynamic Motion Detection, and Tracking and Re-Identification (ReID). Up to 99% of false alarms are filtered before they ever reach an operator. Teams can scale operations without proportional headcount increases, and real incidents are surfaced faster.
Improve Operational Efficiency GCXONE includes built-in tools to help teams move faster:
Z Mode: short compiled alarm summaries for rapid situational awareness
Universal Search: find alarms, devices, sites, and customers instantly
Role-based dashboards: each user sees tailored views and KPIs relevant to their responsibilities
Increase Uptime with Health Check Health Check continuously monitors every connected device and detects issues before they impact operations:
Camera going offline or losing connectivity
Black screen detection
Obstruction detection
Low-light condition alerts
Enable Scalable Growth GCXONE is designed to grow with your business:
Bulk Import: rapidly onboard tenants, customers, sites, and devices at scale
Tower Guard: centralized monitoring for mobile tower infrastructure
Marketplace: instant activation of new features and services without redeployment
Genie AI Assistant Genie is GCXONE's built-in AI assistant. It sits as a floating panel on the dashboard, always available. Users type their question and Genie pulls the relevant data or takes the action directly. Genie handles five key areas:
Insights: alarm summaries and system data analysis
Ticket Management: open, close, or check ticket status by number
VPN Requests: submit and manage VPN requests directly through the assistant
Device Setup and Troubleshooting: step-by-step guidance for configuration and connectivity issues
Spawn: initiate or trigger platform actions quickly
Platform Architecture
Multi-Tenant SaaS and Hierarchy GCXONE runs as a secure, cloud-native multi-tenant platform with strict data isolation per tenant. The platform follows a structured hierarchy:
Tenant, Customer, Site, Device, Sensor This structure enables granular ownership, permissions, and reporting across complex, distributed environments.
Multi-Level Operational Control GCXONE provides visibility and control at every level:
Service Provider: global fleet, SLAs, and operations overview
Customer: customer-specific dashboards and reporting
Site: local operations, incidents, and device status
Device: per-camera and per-sensor health and events
Role-Aware by Design Each user sees only the modules, entities, and dashboards relevant to their role. Access is controlled through structured role configurations and entity-level permissions, ensuring operational clarity, security, and focus for every user group.
Integration with Talos GCXONE works in tandem with Talos as the structured operator workflow layer. The two systems are complementary:
GCXONE pre-processes and filters alarms using NOVA99x AI before anything reaches an operator
Only verified, prioritized alarms are forwarded to Talos, arriving with full context including GIF preview, site info, and device reference

title: Bandwidth Requirements
excerpt:
Bandwidth Requirements Reference Proper bandwidth planning is essential for reliable video surveillance. This reference provides the formulas and metrics needed to calculate capacity for your GCXONE deployment.
Calculation Formula Use this formula to
content:
Bandwidth Requirements Reference Proper bandwidth planning is essential for reliable video surveillance. This reference provides the formulas and metrics needed to calculate capacity for your GCXONE deployment.
Calculation Formula Use this formula to estimate the total bandwidth required for your installation:
Total Bandwidth =
(Number of Cameras × Avg Bitrate) + (Concurrent Viewers × Streaming Bitrate) + 30% Management Overhead
Bitrate Guidelines Bandwidth consumption varies significantly based on resolution and the codec used. H.265 (HEVC) is highly recommended for 50% bandwidth savings. Installation Examples
Small (10 Cameras)
Resolution: 1080p (H.265)
Upload Required: ~30 Mbps
Viewers: 2 Concurrent Streams
Total Recommended: 50 Mbps Symmetric
Medium (50 Cameras)
Resolution: Mixed 1080p/4MP (H.265)
Upload Required: ~200 Mbps
Viewers: 10 Concurrent Streams
Total Recommended: 400 Mbps Symmetric
Large (200+ Cameras)
Resolution: Mixed (H.265)
Upload Required: ~1 Gbps
Total Recommended: 2 Gbps Dedicated Link
Optimization Strategies
Use H.265 Everywhere: Ensure your cameras and NVRs are set to HEVC/H.265 to cut data costs.
Motion-Based Bitrate: Configure Variable Bitrate (VBR) so the stream only spikes during actual activity.
Sub-Streaming: Use a lower resolution "Sub-Stream" for multi-camera grid views, and only switch to "Main Stream" for full-screen inspection.
VLAN Segmentation: Keep video traffic on a dedicated VLAN to prevent congestion with general office data.
Common Issues
Freezing/Artifacts: Usually caused by sustained upload congestion. Try reducing the frame rate from 30 fps to 15 fps.
Delayed Alarms: Often a result of network saturation. Implement Quality of Service (QoS) to prioritize alarm handshake traffic (Port 443).

title: Browser Errors
excerpt:
Resolving Browser Cookie Errors If you can access the Genesis Dashboard but receive an "Oops something went wrong" message when clicking on Alarm Management or Workflows, the issue is almost certainly your browser's security settings.
The Root Cause
content:
Resolving Browser Cookie Errors If you can access the Genesis Dashboard but receive an "Oops something went wrong" message when clicking on Alarm Management or Workflows, the issue is almost certainly your browser's security settings.
The Root Cause Talos is an embedded application. It runs inside the Genesis interface but communicates with a separate security backend. To maintain your session across these two domains, your browser must allow third-party cookies.
️ How to Fix: Quick Toggle The fastest way to fix this is using the address bar toggles:
Look at the right side of your Browser Address Bar.
Click the Eye icon (️) or Cookie icon.
Toggle the switch to "Allow Third-Party Cookies" for this site.
Refresh the page.
️ Manual Browser Settings If the quick toggle isn't visible, use the manual settings for your specific browser:
Go to Settings → Privacy and Security → Third-party cookies. 2. Select Allow third-party cookies. 3. Alternatively: Add [.]nxgen.cloud and [.]evalink.io to the "Allowed to use third-party cookies" list. 1. Go to Settings → Cookies and site permissions. 2. Ensure "Allow sites to save and read cookie data" is On. 3. Ensure "Block third-party cookies" is Off. 1. Go to Settings (or Preferences) → Privacy. 2. Uncheck "Prevent cross-site tracking". 3. Uncheck "Block all cookies". Troubleshooting Checklist
Incognito Mode: If Talos works in Incognito, clear your regular browser cache.
Ad-Blockers: Some ad-blockers (like uBlock Origin) treat Talos cookies as "trackers." Whitelist the site in your extension settings.
Corporate VPN: Some VPNs strip cookie headers. Try connecting from a direct internet line to rule out network-level blocking.

title: Image & Video Display Issues
excerpt:
️ Image & Video Display Issues This guide provides a systematic approach to resolving common visual artifacts, missing alarm snapshots, and streaming failures in the GCXONE platform.
Missing Alarm Snapshots If alarms are triggered but the Video Activ
content:
️ Image & Video Display Issues This guide provides a systematic approach to resolving common visual artifacts, missing alarm snapshots, and streaming failures in the GCXONE platform.
Missing Alarm Snapshots If alarms are triggered but the Video Activity buffer shows empty image slots, verify the following:
Check Time Synchronization Discrepancies between the device clock and GCXONE (even 5-10 seconds) are the primary cause of image retrieval failure.
Ensure the device is using time1.nxgen.cloud or pool.ntp.org.
DST Check: Ensure Daylight Saving Time is toggled ON. Without this, the system may fail during biannual time shifts.
Verify Recording Duration Short recording buffers can cause retrieval misses.
Recommended Setting: Configure your device to record at least 30 seconds of video per event (not 10 seconds). This provides enough "padding" to capture three reliable snapshots even if clocks are slightly out of sync.
Storage Health (HDD Failure) Check the device dashboard for HDD Failure or Disk Full alerts.
If the hard drive is failing, the device will stop buffering video, and all subsequent alarms will have "Empty" image evidence.
Incorrect/Mixed Alarm Images In rare cases, an alarm from "Site A" may display an image from "Site B." This is usually a symptom of Central Cache Corruption.
Scope Check: Determine if the issue affects a single camera or the entire site.
Root Cause: If images are routinely swapped across different physical NVRs, the local metadata index or the cloud cache index is likely out of sync.
Resolution: Document the affected sites and escalate to the Technical Systems Team for a cache-flush operation.
Streaming Failures (Local Mode) If "Cloud Mode" works but "Local Mode" fails to load live video:
H.265 vs H.264 GCXONE cloud mode handles H.265 transcoding server-side. However, Local Mode sends raw packets directly from the Edge device to your browser.
Check Encoding: Verify if the camera is set to H.265.
Install Codec: Ensure your Windows operating system has the H.265 video extensions installed.
Switch to H.264: If the client machine cannot be updated, switch the camera's sub-stream encoding to H.264 for maximum compatibility.
IP Camera Limitations Independent IP cameras have limited processing power and often restrict the number of concurrent RTSP connections.
If multiple operators are viewing the same IP camera, the device may refuse further connections.
Solution: Switch the device to Cloud Only Mode. This routes all requests through a single cloud bridge, bypassing the device's connection limit.
Quick Diagnostics Table Symptom Probable Cause Immediate Action Blue/Black Screen H.265 Codec Missing Install extensions or switch to H.264. "No Image" Button Time Sync Drifting Re-sync device via NTP. Site A shows Site B Cache Corruption Escalate to Tech Team. Laggy/Stuttering Low Upload Bandwidth Reduce sub-stream bitrate/FPS.

content:
fc154fa60085f70b9fdae072f246e1b873255dce.png
GCXONE
Maps Feature Guide
Site Configuration & Operational Awareness
Table 1
Table of Contents
Overview The Maps feature in GCXONE provides a unified geospatial view of your entire security estate. Operators can visualise customers, sites, devices, cameras, and sensors on a live satellite or road map — and interact with them without leaving the map screen. Maps is tightly integrated with other GCXONE applications. When used alongside Alarm Handling through the Multi-Monitor feature, operators gain instant location context whenever an alarm is assigned, enabling faster, more informed responses.
Table 2
Part 1 — Site Setup Before a site appears on the map, it must be created and configured with its geographic coordinates. This section walks through the complete setup workflow.
2.1 Site Overview Page Navigate to a site by browsing the customer hierarchy: Customer → Group → Site. The Overview tab is the landing page for any site and gives you a summary of its status.
f0eb343b0b790e594453f567617a46e9f1f2c61c.png Figure 1 — Site Overview page for "Map Demo" showing Basic Info, Location Info, and Site statistics. The Overview page displays:
Basic Info — contact number for the site.
Location Info — country, city, postal code, and street address.
Site statistics — active devices, non-active devices, total devices, and total sensors.
Table 3
2.2 Editing Site Details Click the Edit button (top-right of the Overview page) to open the Edit Site dialog. The dialog has two tabs: Edit and Properties.
Contact Information The Contact Information section within the Edit tab allows you to capture the site's email address and phone number.
3624fdd5bf7676a18af9d0c263f6c5b9d09f33bd.png Figure 2 — Edit Site dialog showing the Contact Information and Location Details sections.
Location Details The Location Details section is where you set the site's physical coordinates. Fill in the following fields:
Table 4 bc1241ed133d8ad9be54beac60c18c3ff6a96941.png
Figure 3 — Location Details filled in with latitude 31.9489113 and longitude 35.8928442.
Table 5
2.3 Accessing the Map View The global Map view is accessible from the left-hand navigation bar. It displays all customers and their sites as pins on a world map.
028cbebfba7fed1ceb1ac720033bad180160d190.png Figure 4 — Map view with the search bar open. Sites without coordinates set will not appear in search results. Use the search bar at the top-left of the map to locate a specific site by name. As you type, matching site names are suggested in a dropdown. Selecting a result pans the map to that site.
Table 6 8c10416524f2b76463202cd95ba80c27352269aa.png Figure 5 — Map view zoomed to Amman, Jordan, with "Map Demo" appearing as a search suggestion after typing "ma". The map supports two view modes:
Map — standard road map with street names and labels.
Satellite — high-resolution aerial imagery for precise site placement.
Part 2 — Map Edit Mode Edit Mode allows operators and administrators to enrich the map with site-specific overlays: cameras, IO sensors, towers, zone boundaries, and text labels. These overlays persist on the map and give control room staff at-a-glance situational awareness.

title: Platform Architecture Overview
excerpt:
NXGEN is built on a modern, scalable architecture designed for reliability and performance.
Core Components The platform consists of several key components that work together:
content:
NXGEN is built on a modern, scalable architecture designed for reliability and performance.
Core Components The platform consists of several key components that work together:

title: Alarm Flow
excerpt:
Alarm Flow Once an event is classified as a Real Alarm, it enters the alarm flow — the structured process that ensures every genuine security threat is received, assigned, and resolved by the right operator at the right time.
Alarm Trigger An alarm i
content:
Alarm Flow Once an event is classified as a Real Alarm, it enters the alarm flow — the structured process that ensures every genuine security threat is received, assigned, and resolved by the right operator at the right time.
Alarm Trigger An alarm is triggered when one of the following occurs: A camera or sensor detects a real security event that passes Nova 99x AI filtering. A Health Check failure is detected, such as a camera going offline, obstruction, black screen, or low light condition. A manual alarm is triggered by an operator or system administrator.
Alarm Delivery Confirmed alarms are delivered to the operator queue in Talos in real time. Each alarm arrives with full context including the customer and site name, device and sensor reference, event timestamp, alarm classification, and a GIF preview or video clip for fast review.
Operator Queue Inside Talos, operators see a live queue of incoming alarms. Each alarm in the queue can be assigned to a specific operator, preventing duplicate handling. The queue displays who is currently working on each alarm, making team coordination visible at a glance.
Alarm Review The operator opens the alarm and reviews the available information: GIF Preview: A short animated preview of the event for instant context. Jump Back: Replay the 30 seconds before the alarm was triggered. Live View: Switch to a live stream of the camera in real time. Playback: Review recorded footage around the event. Area Masking: Apply a temporary or permanent mask to a specific zone if needed.
Alarm Actions After reviewing the alarm, the operator takes one of the following actions: Confirm as Real: Escalate the alarm and trigger the configured workflow — such as calling a technician, sending an SMS or email, or contacting the customer. Mark as False: Close the alarm as a false positive. This data feeds back into Nova 99x analytics. Escalate: Assign the alarm to a supervisor or higher-level operator.
Workflow Automation GCXONE integrates with Talos workflows to automate alarm response. Depending on the alarm type and site configuration, workflows can automatically notify the customer, dispatch a technician, send an SMS or email alert, escalate to a supervisor, or log the response for SLA reporting.
Alarm Resolution & Audit Every alarm is closed with a resolution note and stored in the Audit Log with full details including the operator who handled it, the time of action, the steps taken, and the final resolution. This data is available for reporting, SLA validation, and compliance audits.

title: Cloud Architecture
excerpt:
Cloud Architecture GCXONE is built on modern cloud-native principles to provide a unified Video Surveillance as a Service (VSaaS) and IoT security management ecosystem.
The Core VSaaS Model GCXONE operates entirely in the cloud, eliminating the compl
content:
Cloud Architecture GCXONE is built on modern cloud-native principles to provide a unified Video Surveillance as a Service (VSaaS) and IoT security management ecosystem.
The Core VSaaS Model GCXONE operates entirely in the cloud, eliminating the complexity of traditional on-premise systems. By shifting to a SaaS model, organizations gain agility, scalability, and freedom from hardware maintenance. No Local Servers: Eliminates dedicated local NVR/DVR maintenance. Infinite Scalability: Add sites and cameras instantly without hitting physical hardware limits. Centralized Management: Deploy security patches and features globally in seconds.
Infrastructure Stack (AWS) The platform is hosted on Amazon Web Services (AWS) using a containerized microservices architecture managed by Kubernetes (EKS). Compute & Orchestration Private EKS Clusters: Core application logic runs on secure, private worker nodes. Auto-Scaling: Node groups adjust resources in real-time based on traffic and processing load. Messaging: Uses Amazon MQ for asynchronous event synchronization across the global platform. Storage & Data Asset Storage: Snapshots and icons are stored in encrypted S3 Buckets. Metadata Management: Uses scalable NoSQL databases for lightning-fast camera metadata retrieval.
The Universal Translator (Proxy Layer) The Proxy Architecture is GCXONE's core innovation. It allows the platform to communicate with diverse hardware manufacturers — Hikvision, Dahua, Axis, and others — by translating their proprietary protocols into a standardized unified command set. The flow works as follows: Diverse hardware communicates via SDK or ONVIF to the Proxy Layer, which acts as a translation engine. The Proxy Layer converts this into a standardized stream using WebRTC or RTSP, which then feeds into the GCXONE Core for AI processing and display. The Proxy Layer removes the need for expensive hardware bridges. Your cameras talk directly to the cloud via the GCXONE gateway.
Multi-Tenant Logical Hierarchy Data isolation is enforced through a strict multi-tenant model. Each organization lives in a dedicated Tenant space, ensuring data privacy and granular access control. The hierarchy is structured as follows: Tenant: The monitoring station or master organization. Customer: Individual client organizations under the tenant. Site: Physical facilities such as offices or warehouses. Device: NVRs or Gateways located at the site. Sensor: Individual camera channels or PIR sensors connected to a device.
Security & Compliance Security is integrated at the architectural level, not added as a bolt-on feature. Encryption: Data at rest and in transit is secured with AES-256. Access Gating: Jump Servers and private VPCs isolate infrastructure from the public web. Authentication: Integrated with Auth0 for enterprise-grade MFA and SSO. Continuous Audit: Every system call and operator action is logged in an immutable audit trail.

title: Event Processing
excerpt: Event Processing Understanding how GCXONE processes events is essential for operators and administrators. Every alarm you see on the dashboard has passed through a structured processing pipeline — from the moment a camera detects movement to the poin
content:
Event Processing Understanding how GCXONE processes events is essential for operators and administrators. Every alarm you see on the dashboard has passed through a structured processing pipeline — from the moment a camera detects movement to the point an operator takes action.
Event Lifecycle Every event in GCXONE follows a defined lifecycle: Detection: A camera or sensor detects an activity and sends a signal to the GCXONE platform via the Proxy Layer. Ingestion: The platform receives the raw event and logs it with a timestamp, device ID, site, and customer reference. AI Analysis: The event is passed through the Nova 99x AI engine, which classifies it as a Real Alarm, False Alarm, or Technical Event. Routing: Confirmed real alarms are routed to the operator queue via Talos for action. Resolution: The operator reviews, processes, and closes the event. All actions are logged in the Audit Trail.
Event Types GCXONE classifies every incoming event into one of three categories: Real Alarm: A genuine security event that requires operator attention, such as motion detection, line crossing, or intrusion. False Alarm: An event triggered by non-threatening activity such as animals, weather, or lighting changes. Filtered out by Nova 99x to reduce operator workload. Technical Event: A system-generated event triggered by Health Check, such as a camera going offline, a black screen, obstruction detection, or low light conditions.
AI Processing — Nova 99x Before any event reaches an operator, it passes through Nova 99x — GCXONE's AI filtering engine. Nova 99x analyzes each event using behavioral analysis and image recognition to determine whether it represents a real threat. This process reduces false alarm volume by up to 99%, freeing operators to focus only on events that matter. Filtering can be applied across thermal and optical image types.
Event Routing Once classified, events are routed based on their type and priority: Real Alarms are pushed to the operator queue in Talos, where they can be assigned, reviewed, and actioned according to configured workflows. False Alarms are filtered out and stored for reporting and analytics. They do not reach the operator queue. Technical Events trigger automated notifications and can initiate workflows such as calling a technician, sending an SMS or email, or escalating to a supervisor.
Event States Every event moves through the following states during its lifecycle: New: Event received and pending review. Filtered: Classified as a false alarm by Nova 99x and removed from the operator queue. Assigned: Allocated to a specific operator for action. In Progress: Operator is actively reviewing the event. Processed: Event has been reviewed and closed.
Event Data & Audit Every event is stored with full metadata including timestamp, device and sensor reference, site and customer, event type and classification, operator actions, and resolution notes. This data is available for reporting, SLA validation, and compliance audits through the Audit Log.

title: GCXONE & Talos Interaction
excerpt: GCXONE & Talos Interaction GCXONE acts as the Intelligent Processing Layer for Talos. While Talos handles core alarm distribution and operator workflows, GCXONE provides the computer vision and AI needed to verify threats, manage video streams, and s
content:
GCXONE & Talos Interaction GCXONE acts as the Intelligent Processing Layer for Talos. While Talos handles core alarm distribution and operator workflows, GCXONE provides the computer vision and AI needed to verify threats, manage video streams, and synchronize site metadata across the ecosystem.
Automatic Site Synchronization When an administrator creates a site in GCXONE, the platform automatically generates a corresponding record in Talos via MQTT messaging. Bidirectional Sync: Updates to site names, addresses, or contact details in GCXONE are pushed to Talos in real time. Test Mode Intelligence: If a site in Talos is set to Test Mode during maintenance, GCXONE automatically disarms that site to prevent unnecessary AI processing.
The Alarm Lifecycle The most critical interaction between GCXONE and Talos occurs during a security event. The flow works as follows: Detection: A hardware device detects a breach and sends a signal to Talos. Forwarding: Talos uses a pre-configured workflow to forward images or video clips to the GCXONE AI engine. Verification: GCXONE's AI classifies the event based on configured priority, white, and black lists. Non-threatening movements such as shadows or animals are filtered out. Response: The operator receives a verified alarm in Talos, complete with bounding boxes showing exactly where the threat was detected. GCXONE's AI reduces false alarms by approximately 80%, ensuring operators focus only on genuine security threats.
Key Automation Workflows Arm/Disarm Schedules: GCXONE can automate the arming and disarming of sites based on client business hours. When the schedule reaches the arming time, GCXONE calls the device API and waits 30 seconds for a status confirmation. If the device fails to arm, an Arming Failure alert is sent to the operator. Event Overflow Protection: To prevent system overload from faulty sensors, GCXONE implements overflow protection. If 25 or more alarms are received from a single sensor within 5 minutes, alarms are temporarily discarded and a maintenance ticket is generated automatically.

title: Getting to Know Talos
excerpt:
Getting to Know Talos Evalink Talos is the primary interface where operators handle alarms and managers organize their customer base. This guide walks you through the core navigation and management concepts.
Architecture & Reliability Talos is built
content:
Getting to Know Talos Evalink Talos is the primary interface where operators handle alarms and managers organize their customer base. This guide walks you through the core navigation and management concepts.
Architecture & Reliability Talos is built on a cloud-native microservices architecture. Every feature — from alarm processing to site management — runs as an independent service on Amazon Web Services (AWS). Global Redundancy: Servers are distributed across America, Europe, and Asia. Fail-Safe Processing: If a specific microservice encounters an issue, the rest of the platform continues to operate without interruption. Silent Updates: Security patches and feature releases are deployed remotely. When a major change occurs, you will see a prompt to reload the page to apply the update immediately.
Navigating the Dashboard When you log in, you are greeted with the Main Dashboard providing a high-level overview of your security health. Alarm Queue: Real-time view of incoming alerts. My Assigned Alarms: Tracks incidents currently under your review. What's New: Click your Profile Icon then What's New to see the latest feature releases and security patches.
Understanding Sites in Talos In Talos, a Site is any entity that can generate an alarm — a building, a specific floor, or even a personal panic button. Each site has one of three statuses: Active: Fully operational. Alarms are processed and recorded. Inactive: No alarms are accepted. Inactive sites do not count toward billing. Test Mode: Used during maintenance. Motion alerts can be ignored while keeping fire alarms active.
Organizing Your Workspace As your organization grows, use Talos grouping features to keep data structured: Organizational Units: The highest level of grouping, such as a regional division. Site Groups: Regional or functional clusters, such as a group of retail locations in a specific area. Service Companies: Link external technicians or guard services to specific sites for automated task routing.
Creating a Site in Talos Go to the Sites page and click Create Site. Choose an ID method — enter a descriptive name manually such as London HQ, or click Sequence to have Talos generate a unique numeric ID automatically. This is useful for large deployments. Scroll to the Address pane and enter the physical location. Ensure the address matches what is configured in GCXONE for accurate synchronization. Optionally add custom fields such as License Plate Number or Site Manager Cell to use in automated workflows.
Virtual Devices & Connections The Virtual Devices tab lists all receivers and connection points in the Talos cloud. Green Icon: Healthy connection. Red Icon: Disconnected hardware. Dual Path (DP): High-security devices using both Ethernet and mobile or cellular backups for redundancy.

title: Tenant-Customer-Site-Device Model
excerpt: Tenant-Customer-Site-Device Model The GCXONE platform uses a 5-level hierarchical structure to organize security assets, manage access control, and ensure data isolation. Understanding this hierarchy is essential for effective platform administration
content:
Tenant-Customer-Site-Device Model The GCXONE platform uses a 5-level hierarchical structure to organize security assets, manage access control, and ensure data isolation. Understanding this hierarchy is essential for effective platform administration and configuration.
The 5-Level Hierarchy Structure The hierarchy flows from top to bottom as follows: Tenant → Customer → Site → Device → Camera/Sensor. Each level is nested inside the one above it, creating a clean organizational structure that enforces data isolation at every layer.
Level 1: Tenant The Tenant is the top-level organization with completely isolated data and configuration. Complete Data Isolation: Each tenant operates in its own dedicated space. Independent Configuration: Custom settings, branding, and integrations per tenant. Subdomain Access: Each tenant gets a unique subdomain such as company.nxgen.cloud. Multi-Customer Support: A single tenant can serve multiple customers, making it ideal for the MSSP model. Common use cases include Managed Security Service Providers (MSSPs), large enterprises with multiple divisions, and service providers offering white-label solutions.
Level 2: Customer The Customer represents individual clients or sub-organizations within a tenant. Client Organization: A distinct organization or business unit under the tenant. Isolated Data: Customer data is completely separate from other customers. Custom Configuration: Site-specific settings and device configurations per customer. User Management: Customer-level user accounts and permissions. Common use cases include individual businesses served by an MSSP, different divisions within a large enterprise, and separate legal entities under a parent organization.
Level 3: Site A Site is a physical location such as a building, facility, or geographic area. Physical Location: Includes a real-world address and geographic coordinates. Talos Synchronization: Sites automatically sync with Talos CMS via MQTT messaging. Site names, addresses, contact info, coordinates, and configuration parameters are kept consistent in real time with bidirectional synchronization and error handling. Device Grouping: All devices at a location are grouped under their site. Address Mapping: Used for mapping, routing, and location services. Note: To delete a site, you must be at the Customer level.
Level 4: Device A Device is the security hardware at a site — such as an NVR, DVR, alarm panel, or IoT gateway. Device types supported: NVR (IP camera recording), DVR (analog camera recording), Alarm Panels (intrusion detection), and IoT Gateways (smart sensors). Each device requires the following configuration: a unique Server Unit ID, IP address and port, authentication credentials, and NTP configuration. Important: Each device must have a unique Server Unit ID. Duplicate IDs cause alarm attribution conflicts.
Level 5: Camera / Sensor The Camera or Sensor is the individual channel connected to a device. Sensor types include video cameras, motion sensors, door sensors, and IoT sensors. Individual Channel: Each sensor occupies a separate channel on its parent device. Automatic Discovery: Sensors are discovered automatically when a device is added. Alarm Attribution: Each sensor can independently trigger alarms and events. Capabilities per sensor may include live stream, playback, PTZ control, and analytics such as motion detection and line crossing.
Common Hierarchy Patterns Single Organization: One tenant contains one customer, which contains one or more sites. Multi-Location Business: One tenant contains one customer with multiple sites — ideal for retail chains or enterprises with branch offices. MSSP Model: One tenant contains multiple customers, each with their own sites and devices — the standard model for managed security service providers.
Integration with Talos CMS GCXONE and Talos are automatically synchronized by default. Sites created in GCXONE appear directly in Talos. Alarms are attributed to the correct site and device, and events are correlated based on hierarchy relationships. If a specific site is not fully synced, you can manually force synchronization from the site actions menu using Push to Talos (sends data from GCXONE to Talos) or Pull from Talos (retrieves data from Talos to GCXONE).
Best Practices Use clear, consistent naming conventions across all levels. Ensure site addresses are accurate for Talos synchronization. Always assign unique Server Unit IDs to avoid alarm attribution conflicts. Organize sites and devices logically to reflect real-world structure. Document tenant-specific configurations for future reference. Avoid reusing Server Unit IDs, using vague names, skipping address validation, or ignoring hierarchy when setting permissions.
